Setup the Project Path¶

In [56]:
import sys
from pathlib import Path

# Add project root (parent of notebooks/) to Python path so "import src" works
PROJECT_ROOT = Path.cwd().parent  # .../EC334_Summative
if str(PROJECT_ROOT) not in sys.path:
    sys.path.insert(0, str(PROJECT_ROOT))
In [57]:
# Performing necessary imports
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

import statsmodels.api as sm

from src.dataset import build_dataset  # this should already work in your repo

# Checking train and test data
data = build_dataset()
df_full  = data["full"].copy()
df_train = data["train"].copy()
df_test  = data["test"].copy()

print("Shapes:", df_full.shape, df_train.shape, df_test.shape)
print("Train period:", df_train["period_q"].min(), "to", df_train["period_q"].max())
print("Test period :", df_test["period_q"].min(), "to", df_test["period_q"].max())

Shapes: (224, 67) (208, 67) (16, 67)
Train period: 1968Q1 to 2019Q4
Test period : 2020Q1 to 2023Q4

Quick Sanity Check¶

In [58]:
y_var = "pi_pce_yoy"
x_vars = ["exp_mich_1y_mean", "u_gap_nroust"]

need_cols = ["period_q", "date", y_var] + x_vars
missing = [c for c in need_cols if c not in df_full.columns]
print("Missing columns:", missing)

# Missingness summary (train/test)
for c in [y_var] + x_vars:
    print(f"{c:>20} | train NA={df_train[c].isna().sum():>4} | test NA={df_test[c].isna().sum():>2}")

Checking which test quarter is missing¶

dtest = df_test[[y_var] + x_vars + ["period_q", "date"]].copy()

missing_rows = dtest[dtest[x_vars].isna().any(axis=1)]
print("Missing RHS rows in test:")

Analysis Section 1: Baseline + Expectations Horse Race (OOS 2020Q1 - 2023Q3)¶

In [61]:
TEST_END = pd.Period("2023Q3", freq="Q")

df_test_common = df_test[df_test["period_q"] <= TEST_END].copy()

print("Original test window:", df_test["period_q"].min(), "to", df_test["period_q"].max(), "| N =", len(df_test))
print("Common test window  :", df_test_common["period_q"].min(), "to", df_test_common["period_q"].max(), "| N =", len(df_test_common))

# Quick check: confirm we're not carrying missing unemployment into the evaluation set
print("Missing UNRATE in original test:", df_test["UNRATE"].isna().sum())
print("Missing UNRATE in common test  :", df_test_common["UNRATE"].isna().sum())

Original test window: 2020Q1 to 2023Q4 | N = 16
Common test window  : 2020Q1 to 2023Q3 | N = 15
Missing UNRATE in original test: 1
Missing UNRATE in common test  : 0
  • Establish a Common out-of-sample evaluation window (2020Q1–2023Q3).
  • The merged dataset includes 2023Q4 for some series but not for core Phillips-curve inputs (e.g., UNRATE).
  • To keep out-of-sample forecast comparisons consistent across specifications, we evaluate all models on a common support window ending in 2023Q3.

Utility helpers¶

In [62]:
def fit_ols_hac(df, y, x, hac_lags=4):
    """
    Fit OLS with Newey–West (HAC) standard errors.
    
    Parameters
    ----------
    df : DataFrame
        Estimation sample (typically df_train).
    y : str
        Dependent variable name.
    x : list[str]
        List of regressor names.
    hac_lags : int
        Max lags used for HAC covariance (quarterly data: 4 is a common choice).
        
    Returns
    -------
    res : statsmodels RegressionResults
        Fitted model with HAC robust covariance.
    d_used : DataFrame
        The estimation dataframe after dropping missing values.
    """
    d_used = df[[y] + x + ["period_q", "date"]].dropna().copy()
    X = sm.add_constant(d_used[x])
    Y = d_used[y]
    res = sm.OLS(Y, X).fit(cov_type="HAC", cov_kwds={"maxlags": hac_lags})
    return res, d_used


def oos_eval(df_train, df_test_eval, y, x, hac_lags=4):
    """
    Out-of-sample (OOS) evaluation:
    1) Estimate on df_train (pre-2020).
    2) Predict on df_test_eval (2020Q1–2023Q3) using realized RHS variables (static/conditional forecast).
    3) Compute forecast performance metrics (RMSE/MAE/Bias).
    
    Returns
    -------
    out : dict
        Contains:
          - res: fitted model
          - dtrain: estimation dataframe used
          - dtest: test dataframe used with columns [y, yhat, err]
          - metrics: dict with train_N, test_N, rmse, mae, bias
    """
    res, dtrain = fit_ols_hac(df_train, y, x, hac_lags=hac_lags)

    dtest = df_test_eval[[y] + x + ["period_q", "date"]].dropna().copy()
    Xtest = sm.add_constant(dtest[x], has_constant="add")

    dtest["yhat"] = res.predict(Xtest)
    dtest["err"] = dtest["yhat"] - dtest[y]

    metrics = {
        "train_N": int(len(dtrain)),
        "test_N": int(len(dtest)),
        "rmse": float(np.sqrt(np.mean(dtest["err"] ** 2))),
        "mae":  float(np.mean(np.abs(dtest["err"]))),
        "bias": float(np.mean(dtest["err"]))
    }

    return {"res": res, "dtrain": dtrain, "dtest": dtest, "metrics": metrics}

Utility helpers (to keep the notebook readable). We will estimate many Phillips-curve variants (different expectations measures, slack measures, and supply controls). To avoid repeating boilerplate, we define two helper functions:

  • fit_ols_hac() estimates an OLS Phillips curve with Newey–West (HAC) standard errors (useful for quarterly macro data with serial correlation).

  • oos_eval() implements the core experiment: fit on the pre-2020 training sample, then generate static/conditional out-of-sample predictions for 2020Q1–2023Q3 by plugging in realized RHS variables, and compute RMSE/MAE/bias.

Returning the test dataframe with yhat and errors makes plotting and comparison across specifications straightforward.

In [63]:
# Baseline specification
y_var = "pi_pce_yoy"
x_baseline = ["exp_mich_1y_mean", "u_gap_nroust"]

baseline = oos_eval(df_train, df_test_common, y_var, x_baseline, hac_lags=4)

print("Baseline spec:", f"{y_var} ~ " + " + ".join(x_baseline))
print("OOS metrics (2020Q1–2023Q3):", baseline["metrics"])
print("\nCoefficients (HAC SEs):")
print(baseline["res"].summary())

Baseline spec: pi_pce_yoy ~ exp_mich_1y_mean + u_gap_nroust
OOS metrics (2020Q1–2023Q3): {'train_N': 204, 'test_N': 15, 'rmse': 0.6331462645089453, 'mae': 0.5641125460565358, 'bias': 0.2666666908468941}

Coefficients (HAC SEs):
                            OLS Regression Results                            
==============================================================================
Dep. Variable:             pi_pce_yoy   R-squared:                       0.817
Model:                            OLS   Adj. R-squared:                  0.816
Method:                 Least Squares   F-statistic:                     98.04
Date:                Mon, 26 Jan 2026   Prob (F-statistic):           1.93e-30
Time:                        13:16:08   Log-Likelihood:                -305.52
No. Observations:                 204   AIC:                             617.0
Df Residuals:                     201   BIC:                             627.0
Df Model:                           2                                         
Covariance Type:                  HAC                                         
====================================================================================
                       coef    std err          z      P>|z|      [0.025      0.975]
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
const               -1.9139      0.356     -5.372      0.000      -2.612      -1.216
exp_mich_1y_mean     1.1531      0.082     14.002      0.000       0.992       1.314
u_gap_nroust        -0.0020      0.105     -0.019      0.985      -0.207       0.203
==============================================================================
Omnibus:                       71.651   Durbin-Watson:                   0.788
Prob(Omnibus):                  0.000   Jarque-Bera (JB):              494.922
Skew:                           1.137   Prob(JB):                    3.38e-108
Kurtosis:                      10.284   Cond. No.                         13.9
==============================================================================

Notes:
[1] Standard Errors are heteroscedasticity and autocorrelation robust (HAC) using 4 lags and without small sample correction

Baseline out-of-sample test. We start with an expectations-augmented Phillips curve estimated on the pre-pandemic sample (1968Q1–2019Q4): $$\pi_t \;=\; \alpha \;+\; \beta\,\pi^e_t \;+\; \kappa\,(u_t - u_t^{*}) \;+\; \varepsilon_t.$$ Here, inflation is PCE y/y (pi_pce_yoy), expectations are Michigan 1-year median (exp_mich_1y_median), and slack is the unemployment gap using NROUST (u_gap_nroust, i.e. $u_t-u_t^{*}$).

We then evaluate whether the pre-2020 coefficients predict inflation out-of-sample over 2020Q1–2023Q3 using a static/conditional forecast (i.e., we plug in realized values of $\pi^e_t$ and $u_t-u_t^{*}$).

In [64]:
d = baseline["dtest"]  # contains date, y, yhat, err for the rows used in the OOS evaluation

plt.figure(figsize=(10,4))
plt.plot(d["date"], d[y_var], label="Actual", linewidth=2)
plt.plot(d["date"], d["yhat"], label="Predicted (pre-2020 fit)")
plt.axhline(0, linewidth=1)
plt.title(f"Baseline OOS forecast: {y_var} ~ " + " + ".join(x_baseline))
plt.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Baseline out-of-sample fit (visual).
This plot compares actual post-pandemic inflation $\pi_t$ to the prediction $\hat{\pi}_t$ produced by the pre-2020 estimated Phillips curve.
A systematic and persistent gap (especially in 2021–2022) would be evidence of “failure to predict” in an out-of-sample forecasting sense.

In [65]:
d = baseline["dtest"].copy()

# Cumulative forecast error helps detect systematic under/over-prediction
d["cum_err"] = d["err"].cumsum()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10,4))
ax.plot(d["date"], d["err"], label="Forecast error (yhat - y)")
ax.axhline(0, linewidth=1)
ax.set_title("Baseline OOS forecast error over time")
ax.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

plt.figure(figsize=(10,4))
plt.plot(d["date"], d["cum_err"], label="Cumulative forecast error")
plt.axhline(0, linewidth=1)
plt.title("Baseline cumulative OOS forecast error")
plt.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Baseline forecast errors (what the Phillips curve gets wrong). Define the out-of-sample forecast error as $e_t = \hat{\pi}_t - \pi_t$, where $\hat{\pi}_t$ is predicted using coefficients estimated on the pre-2020 sample and realized post-2020 RHS values.

Phase 1 (2020–early 2021): overprediction. Early in the pandemic window, $e_t > 0$: the model predicts higher inflation than realized inflation. This is consistent with the sharp disinflation in 2020 not being well captured by the baseline mapping from expectations and slack alone (and/or unusual pandemic-era dynamics in prices).

Phase 2 (mid-2021 through 2023): persistent underprediction. From roughly 2021 onwards, $e_t$ turns negative and stays mostly below zero through 2022–23, reaching around $-1.5$ to $-2$ percentage points at its worst. This indicates a systematic failure in the baseline Phillips curve: it does not generate enough inflation during the peak of the post-pandemic surge.

Cumulative error highlights a structural break in performance. The cumulative forecast error $\sum_{s\le t} e_s$ rises during the early overprediction phase (positive cumulative error), then reverses and becomes steadily more negative. By the end of the window, the cumulative error is substantially below zero, implying that the baseline model underpredicts inflation on average over the post-2021 period (the negative errors dominate).

Interpretation (what this does and doesn’t prove). This is a conditional out-of-sample test: we are not forecasting expectations or slack, but instead asking whether the pre-2020 mapping from $(\pi^e_t,; u_t-u^*_t)$ to inflation remains stable after 2020. Persistent negative errors therefore suggest that at least one of the following is true:

the baseline Phillips curve is misspecified for the post-pandemic period (e.g., omitted supply/cost terms),

the relevant expectations measure differs from what is used here (measurement issue), and/or

the slope or intercept changed (instability in coefficients).

This motivates the next specifications: adding observable supply proxies (e.g., energy inflation) and swapping expectations measures to see whether the “failure” is robust or largely a measurement/omitted-variable problem.

In [66]:
# --- Cell F (replacement): Expectations horse race with automatic availability checks ---

slack = "u_gap_nroust"
y_var = "pi_pce_yoy"

def available(varname: str) -> bool:
    """Return True if var exists and has at least one non-missing observation."""
    return (varname in df_full.columns) and df_full[varname].notna().any()

# Candidate expectations proxies (only those available will be used)
expectations_candidates = [
    ("Michigan median", "exp_mich_1y_median"),
    ("Michigan mean",   "exp_mich_1y_mean"),
    ("SPF 1y",          "exp_spf_1y"),
    ("Swap 1y",         "exp_swap_1y"),
]

expectations_list = [(lab, var) for lab, var in expectations_candidates if available(var)]
print("Using expectations series:", expectations_list)

rows = []
preds = {}

for label, exp_var in expectations_list:
    x = [exp_var, slack]
    out = oos_eval(df_train, df_test_common, y_var, x, hac_lags=4)

    m = out["metrics"]
    dtrain_used = out["dtrain"]

    rows.append({
        "expectations": label,
        "exp_var": exp_var,
        "slack": slack,
        "train_start": dtrain_used["period_q"].min(),
        "train_end":   dtrain_used["period_q"].max(),
        "train_N":     m["train_N"],
        "test_start":  out["dtest"]["period_q"].min() if len(out["dtest"]) else None,
        "test_end":    out["dtest"]["period_q"].max() if len(out["dtest"]) else None,
        "test_N":      m["test_N"],
        "rmse":        m["rmse"],
        "mae":         m["mae"],
        "bias":        m["bias"],
    })

    preds[label] = out["dtest"][["date", "period_q", y_var, "yhat"]].copy()

perf = pd.DataFrame(rows).sort_values("rmse")
perf

Using expectations series: [('Michigan median', 'exp_mich_1y_median'), ('Michigan mean', 'exp_mich_1y_mean'), ('SPF 1y', 'exp_spf_1y'), ('Swap 1y', 'exp_swap_1y')]
Out[66]:
expectations exp_var slack train_start train_end train_N test_start test_end test_N rmse mae bias
1 Michigan mean exp_mich_1y_mean u_gap_nroust 1969Q1 2019Q4 204 2020Q1 2023Q3 15 0.633146 0.564113 0.266667
0 Michigan median exp_mich_1y_median u_gap_nroust 1978Q1 2019Q4 168 2020Q1 2023Q3 15 1.192549 1.020345 -0.490756
3 Swap 1y exp_swap_1y u_gap_nroust 2004Q3 2019Q4 62 2020Q1 2023Q3 15 2.003002 1.658197 -1.446655
2 SPF 1y exp_spf_1y u_gap_nroust 1981Q3 2019Q4 154 2020Q1 2023Q3 15 2.487187 2.048549 -1.861308

Expectations measurement horse race (robust to data availability).
Some expectations series have shorter sample coverage (e.g., inflation swaps typically begin in the mid-2000s), and some series may not be present (e.g., Michigan mean vs median depending on the dataset build).
To avoid hard-coding, we include only expectations variables that exist and have non-missing observations.

For each expectations proxy, we estimate the Phillips curve on the available pre-2020 training sample and evaluate out-of-sample prediction performance over the common post-pandemic test window (2020Q1–2023Q3).
We report training sample start/end dates and $N$ to make differences in data coverage transparent.

In [68]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Choose the prediction dataframe with the most rows as the base for "Actual"
base_label = max(preds.keys(), key=lambda k: len(preds[k]))
actual_df = preds[base_label].copy()

plt.figure(figsize=(10,4))

# Actual series
plt.plot(actual_df["date"], actual_df[y_var], label="Actual", linewidth=2)

# Predicted series for each expectations proxy
for label, d in preds.items():
    plt.plot(d["date"], d["yhat"], label=f"Predicted ({label})")

# visual marker around when inflation takes off
try:
    vline_date = df_test_common.loc[df_test_common["period_q"] == pd.Period("2021Q1", freq="Q"), "date"].iloc[0]
    plt.axvline(vline_date, linewidth=1)
except Exception:
    pass

plt.axhline(0, linewidth=1)
plt.title(f"OOS forecasts (to {TEST_END}): {y_var} ~ expectations + {slack}")
plt.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Overlay plot: predicted inflation paths by expectations proxy.
This figure holds the slack measure fixed and varies only the expectations proxy $\pi^e_t$.
Each predicted line uses coefficients estimated on the pre-2020 training sample available for that expectations series (e.g., swaps may imply a shorter training window).
Visual differences, especially during the 2021–2022 inflation surge indicate whether “Phillips curve failure” is primarily about the choice of expectations measure rather than a breakdown of the slack–inflation relationship.

Robustness Checks: Common training window/Michigan mean vs median wedge¶

In [69]:
# --- Robustness 1: Restrict training window to 2004Q1–2019Q4 for ALL expectations measures ---

TRAIN_START_RESTRICTED = pd.Period("2004Q1", freq="Q")
df_train_2004 = df_train[df_train["period_q"] >= TRAIN_START_RESTRICTED].copy()

print("Restricted train window:", df_train_2004["period_q"].min(), "to", df_train_2004["period_q"].max(),
      "| N =", len(df_train_2004))

slack = "u_gap_nroust"
y_var = "pi_pce_yoy"

def available(varname: str) -> bool:
    return (varname in df_full.columns) and df_full[varname].notna().any()

expectations_candidates = [
    ("Michigan median", "exp_mich_1y_median"),
    ("Michigan mean",   "exp_mich_1y_mean"),
    ("SPF 1y",          "exp_spf_1y"),
    ("Swap 1y",         "exp_swap_1y"),
]
expectations_list = [(lab, var) for lab, var in expectations_candidates if available(var)]
print("Using expectations series:", expectations_list)

rows_restricted = []
preds_restricted = {}

for label, exp_var in expectations_list:
    x = [exp_var, slack]
    out = oos_eval(df_train_2004, df_test_common, y_var, x, hac_lags=4)

    m = out["metrics"]
    dtrain_used = out["dtrain"]

    rows_restricted.append({
        "expectations": label,
        "exp_var": exp_var,
        "slack": slack,
        "train_start": dtrain_used["period_q"].min(),
        "train_end":   dtrain_used["period_q"].max(),
        "train_N":     m["train_N"],
        "test_start":  out["dtest"]["period_q"].min() if len(out["dtest"]) else None,
        "test_end":    out["dtest"]["period_q"].max() if len(out["dtest"]) else None,
        "test_N":      m["test_N"],
        "rmse":        m["rmse"],
        "mae":         m["mae"],
        "bias":        m["bias"],
    })

    preds_restricted[label] = out["dtest"][["date", "period_q", y_var, "yhat"]].copy()

perf_restricted = pd.DataFrame(rows_restricted).sort_values("rmse")
perf_restricted

Restricted train window: 2004Q1 to 2019Q4 | N = 64
Using expectations series: [('Michigan median', 'exp_mich_1y_median'), ('Michigan mean', 'exp_mich_1y_mean'), ('SPF 1y', 'exp_spf_1y'), ('Swap 1y', 'exp_swap_1y')]
Out[69]:
expectations exp_var slack train_start train_end train_N test_start test_end test_N rmse mae bias
1 Michigan mean exp_mich_1y_mean u_gap_nroust 2004Q1 2019Q4 64 2020Q1 2023Q3 15 0.718758 0.615468 -0.465068
0 Michigan median exp_mich_1y_median u_gap_nroust 2004Q1 2019Q4 64 2020Q1 2023Q3 15 1.107839 0.933580 -0.933580
2 SPF 1y exp_spf_1y u_gap_nroust 2004Q1 2019Q4 64 2020Q1 2023Q3 15 1.270901 0.899365 -0.526568
3 Swap 1y exp_swap_1y u_gap_nroust 2004Q3 2019Q4 62 2020Q1 2023Q3 15 2.003002 1.658197 -1.446655

Robustness 1: common training window (2004Q1–2019Q4).
Inflation swaps are only available starting in the mid-2000s, so swap-based Phillips curves are estimated on a shorter training sample by construction.
To make expectations proxies comparable, we re-estimate all models on a common restricted training window (2004Q1–2019Q4) and re-rank out-of-sample performance over 2020Q1–2023Q3.

In [99]:
# --- Robustness 2: Michigan mean vs median wedge diagnostics ---

need = ["date", "period_q", "exp_mich_1y_mean", "exp_mich_1y_median"]
missing = [c for c in need if c not in df_full.columns]
print("Missing columns:", missing)

dm = df_full[need].dropna().copy()
dm["wedge_mean_minus_median"] = dm["exp_mich_1y_mean"] - dm["exp_mich_1y_median"]

print("Wedge summary (overall):")
print(dm["wedge_mean_minus_median"].describe())

# Plot wedge over time, zoom on 2018+

dm_recent = dm[dm["period_q"] >= pd.Period("2018Q1", freq="Q")].copy()
plt.figure(figsize=(10,4))
plt.plot(dm_recent["date"], dm_recent["wedge_mean_minus_median"])
plt.axhline(0, linewidth=1)
plt.title("Michigan mean−median wedge (zoom: 2018Q1 onward)")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Missing columns: []
Wedge summary (overall):
count    183.000000
mean       0.895993
std        0.455889
min        0.066667
25%        0.566667
50%        0.800000
75%        1.183333
max        2.566667
Name: wedge_mean_minus_median, dtype: float64

Robustness 2: Michigan mean vs median.
Michigan provides both a mean and a median 1-year inflation expectation. The mean can be influenced by the upper tail of respondents’ beliefs, while the median is more robust to outliers.
To understand why the mean may fit post-2020 inflation better, we plot the wedge $(\text{mean} - \text{median})$ over time and check whether it expands during the inflation surge (2021–2022).

Baseline out-of-sample results (2020Q1–2023Q3): fit, errors, and the role of expectations¶

We begin by estimating an expectations-augmented Phillips curve on the pre-pandemic sample (1968Q1–2019Q4) and evaluating its out-of-sample (OOS) performance over 2020Q1–2023Q3 using a static/conditional forecast design: $$\pi_t = \alpha + \beta\,\pi^e_t + \kappa\,slack_t + \varepsilon_t,$$ where $\pi_t$ is PCE y/y inflation and $slack_t$ is the unemployment gap.

Baseline predicted vs actual (OOS).
The baseline OOS plot shows that the pre-2020 Phillips curve captures the broad direction of inflation dynamics, but it is not tight enough to match the post-pandemic episode: predicted inflation rises and then falls, yet it does not fully track the magnitude of the 2021–2022 surge. In this narrow forecasting sense, the baseline specification “fails to predict” key features of post-pandemic inflation.

Baseline error dynamics.
The forecast-error plot ($e_t=\hat{\pi}_t-\pi_t$) indicates two phases: an initial period of overprediction around 2020–early 2021 ($e_t>0$), followed by persistent underprediction from roughly 2021 onward ($e_t<0$). The cumulative error $\sum_{s\le t} e_s$ rises early and then declines steadily into negative territory, implying that the underprediction during the inflation surge dominates the overall OOS performance. This pattern suggests a systematic miss rather than purely transitory noise.

Expectations horse race (holding slack fixed).
A key result is that the extent of “failure” depends strongly on which proxy is used for expected inflation $\pi^e_t$. Re-estimating the same pre-2020 Phillips curve while swapping only the expectations measure shows that Michigan household expectations (mean and median) deliver the closest tracking of post-pandemic inflation, while SPF professional forecasts and inflation swaps perform worse in the baseline specification. Thus, the apparent breakdown of the Phillips curve is partly an expectations-measurement issue: the same reduced-form relationship can look substantially better or worse depending on how $\pi^e_t$ is measured.

Robustness checks.

  1. Common training window (2004Q1–2019Q4). Because swaps have shorter historical coverage, we re-estimate all expectations specifications on a common restricted training sample. This changes the relative ranking between SPF and swaps (swaps become slightly worse than SPF), but Michigan mean and median remain the best-performing expectations measures and remain very close to each other in RMSE. This indicates the core conclusion is not driven solely by different estimation windows.
  2. Michigan mean vs median wedge. The mean–median wedge is usually modest (around 0.5pp) but spikes sharply during 2021–2022 (roughly 1–2.5pp), consistent with the mean being pulled up by a higher-expectations tail during the surge. This helps rationalize why the mean can fit the post-pandemic episode slightly better than the median.

Interpretation and next step.
Taken together, these baseline results show that a simple pre-2020 Phillips curve does not perfectly predict post-pandemic inflation, but the degree of failure is not invariant: it depends importantly on the expectations proxy and, potentially, on omitted factors. Importantly, these exercises do not yet establish that the unemployment–inflation relationship (the slack coefficient $\kappa$) changed after COVID; they motivate extending the specification (e.g., adding observable supply/cost controls such as energy inflation) and then directly testing whether $\kappa$ is stable in the post-2020 period.

Plot used in paper¶

In [72]:
# ---- Exhibit 1: two-panel expectations tracking plot (REUSE preds + preds_restricted) ----

y_var = "pi_pce_yoy"

# Sanity checks
assert "preds" in globals(), "preds not found. Run Cell 19 first (expectations horse race)."
assert "preds_restricted" in globals(), "preds_restricted not found. Run Cell 24 first (restricted 2004Q1–2019Q4)."

# Helper: pick an 'actual' series from any one of the stored dfs
def get_actual(pred_dict, y_var):
    # pick first entry
    first_key = list(pred_dict.keys())[0]
    d = pred_dict[first_key].copy()
    return d[["date", y_var]].drop_duplicates()

# -------- Panel A: full pre-2020 train, exclude swaps (so it’s “long sample” expectations) --------
actual_A = get_actual(preds, y_var)

panelA_labels = [k for k in preds.keys() if "Swap" not in k]  # exclude swaps for Panel A

# -------- Panel B: restricted train window (2004Q1–2019Q4), include swaps --------
actual_B = get_actual(preds_restricted, y_var)
panelB_labels = list(preds_restricted.keys())  # include Swap if present

# -------- Plot --------
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 4.5), sharey=True)

# Panel A
ax = axes[0]
ax.plot(actual_A["date"], actual_A[y_var], label="Actual PCE inflation (y/y)", linewidth=2)
for lab in panelA_labels:
    ax.plot(preds[lab]["date"], preds[lab]["yhat"], label=f"Predicted ({lab})")
ax.set_title("Panel A: Train ≤ 2019Q4 (no swaps)")
ax.axhline(0, linewidth=1)
ax.legend(fontsize=8)

# Panel B
ax = axes[1]
ax.plot(actual_B["date"], actual_B[y_var], label="Actual PCE inflation (y/y)", linewidth=2)
for lab in panelB_labels:
    ax.plot(preds_restricted[lab]["date"], preds_restricted[lab]["yhat"], label=f"Predicted ({lab})")
ax.set_title("Panel B: Train 2004Q1–2019Q4 (incl swaps)")
ax.axhline(0, linewidth=1)
ax.legend(fontsize=8)

plt.suptitle("Figure 1: Post-pandemic tracking under alternative expectations measures", y=1.02)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Analysis Section 2: Considering the forecasting power of OOS tests¶

Test 1: Benchmark Comparison¶

In [73]:
# --- Benchmark comparison setup ---

# Uses our existing variables:
# y_var (e.g. "pi_pce_yoy"), TEST_END (2023Q3 Period), df_test_common, df_full, df_train
# baseline = oos_eval(...) from earlier

# 1) Build a time-sorted series for inflation to create lag-based benchmarks
ts = df_full[["period_q", "date", y_var]].sort_values("period_q").copy()

ts["pi_l1"] = ts[y_var].shift(1)                      # random-walk benchmark
ts["pi_ma4"] = ts[y_var].shift(1).rolling(4).mean()   # trailing 4-quarter average benchmark

# Restrict to the common test window
ts_test = ts[(ts["period_q"] >= df_test_common["period_q"].min()) &
             (ts["period_q"] <= TEST_END)].copy()

# 2) Merge in baseline Phillips curve predictions (already OOS and dropna-ed)
pc_pred = baseline["dtest"][["period_q", "yhat"]].copy().rename(columns={"yhat": "pi_hat_pc"})
eval_df = ts_test.merge(pc_pred, on="period_q", how="left")

# 3) Keep rows where we have the dependent variable and all benchmark forecasts
# (PC predictions may be missing in a quarter if regressors were missing)
eval_df = eval_df.dropna(subset=[y_var, "pi_hat_pc", "pi_l1", "pi_ma4"]).copy()

print("Evaluation window used:", eval_df["period_q"].min(), "to", eval_df["period_q"].max())
print("N used for benchmark comparison:", len(eval_df))
eval_df.head()

Evaluation window used: 2020Q1 to 2023Q3
N used for benchmark comparison: 15
Out[73]:
period_q date pi_pce_yoy pi_l1 pi_ma4 pi_hat_pc
0 2020Q1 2020-03-31 23:59:59.999999999 1.495308 1.400986 1.437159 1.200827
1 2020Q2 2020-06-30 23:59:59.999999999 0.521810 1.495308 1.441665 1.528293
2 2020Q3 2020-09-30 23:59:59.999999999 1.104420 0.521810 1.201700 1.997811
3 2020Q4 2020-12-31 23:59:59.999999999 1.213169 1.104420 1.130631 1.656025
4 2021Q1 2021-03-31 23:59:59.999999999 2.070571 1.213169 1.083677 2.810217

Benchmark forecasts (why do this?).
Out-of-sample “failure” is more informative when compared to simple inflation-forecast benchmarks.
Here we construct three benchmarks that use only lagged inflation information: a no-change forecast $\hat{\pi}_t=\pi_{t-1}$, a trailing 4-quarter average, and an AR(1) estimated on the pre-2020 training sample.
We then compare these to the baseline Phillips-curve forecast over the common test window (2020Q1–2023Q3).

In [74]:
# --- AR(1) benchmark estimated on pre-2020 train sample ---

# Build lagged inflation in the train sample
train_ts = df_train[["period_q", y_var]].sort_values("period_q").copy()
train_ts["pi_l1"] = train_ts[y_var].shift(1)

train_ar = train_ts.dropna(subset=[y_var, "pi_l1"]).copy()
X_ar = sm.add_constant(train_ar["pi_l1"])
Y_ar = train_ar[y_var]

# Plain OLS is fine for benchmark; HAC also ok (doesn't matter much for forecasting)
ar_res = sm.OLS(Y_ar, X_ar).fit(cov_type="HAC", cov_kwds={"maxlags": 4})
print(ar_res.summary())

# Predict AR(1) on the eval_df rows (needs pi_l1)
eval_df["pi_hat_ar1"] = ar_res.predict(sm.add_constant(eval_df["pi_l1"], has_constant="add"))

                            OLS Regression Results
