Model:                            OLS   Adj. R-squared:                  0.950
Method:                 Least Squares   F-statistic:                     961.0
Date:                Mon, 26 Jan 2026   Prob (F-statistic):           1.61e-78
Time:                        13:19:38   Log-Likelihood:                -172.22
No. Observations:                 203   AIC:                             348.4
Df Residuals:                     201   BIC:                             355.1
Df Model:                           1                                         
Covariance Type:                  HAC                                         
==============================================================================
                 coef    std err          z      P>|z|      [0.025      0.975]
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
const          0.0694      0.087      0.800      0.423      -0.101       0.240
pi_l1          0.9764      0.031     31.000      0.000       0.915       1.038
==============================================================================
Omnibus:                       23.971   Durbin-Watson:                   1.083
Prob(Omnibus):                  0.000   Jarque-Bera (JB):              119.342
Skew:                           0.067   Prob(JB):                     1.22e-26
Kurtosis:                       6.754   Cond. No.                         7.70
==============================================================================

Notes:
[1] Standard Errors are heteroscedasticity and autocorrelation robust (HAC) using 4 lags and without small sample correction

AR(1) benchmark.
We estimate an AR(1) model of inflation on the pre-2020 training sample, $$\pi_t = a + \rho\,\pi_{t-1} + u_t,$$ and generate OOS predictions in the test window using only lagged inflation. This provides a standard low-information benchmark for inflation forecasting.

In [75]:
def forecast_metrics(y_true, y_hat):
    err = y_hat - y_true
    return {
        "RMSE": float(np.sqrt(np.mean(err**2))),
        "MAE":  float(np.mean(np.abs(err))),
        "Bias": float(np.mean(err))
    }

y = eval_df[y_var]

results = []
for name, col in [
    ("Phillips curve (baseline)", "pi_hat_pc"),
    ("Naive no-change (pi_{t-1})", "pi_l1"),
    ("Trailing mean (4q)", "pi_ma4"),
    ("AR(1) benchmark", "pi_hat_ar1"),
]:
    m = forecast_metrics(y, eval_df[col])
    results.append({"model": name, **m})

bench_table = pd.DataFrame(results).sort_values("RMSE")
bench_table
Out[75]:
model RMSE MAE Bias
0 Phillips curve (baseline) 0.633146 0.564113 0.266667
3 AR(1) benchmark 0.856432 0.700365 -0.153881
1 Naive no-change (pi_{t-1}) 0.866855 0.717684 -0.133281
2 Trailing mean (4q) 1.713730 1.402523 -0.449346

Forecasting power in OOS: does the Phillips curve beat simple benchmarks?
RMSE/MAE compare overall accuracy; Bias summarizes systematic under- or over-prediction.
If the baseline Phillips curve does not outperform simple lag-inflation benchmarks, then “failure to predict” may reflect the general difficulty of forecasting inflation rather than a uniquely post-COVID breakdown of the slack–inflation relationship.

In [76]:
plt.figure(figsize=(10,4))
plt.plot(eval_df["date"], eval_df[y_var], label="Actual", linewidth=2)
plt.plot(eval_df["date"], eval_df["pi_hat_pc"], label="Phillips curve (baseline)")
plt.plot(eval_df["date"], eval_df["pi_l1"], label="Naive (pi_{t-1})")
plt.plot(eval_df["date"], eval_df["pi_ma4"], label="Trailing mean (4q)")
plt.plot(eval_df["date"], eval_df["pi_hat_ar1"], label="AR(1)")
plt.axhline(0, linewidth=1)
plt.title(f"OOS benchmark comparison (to {TEST_END}): forecasting {y_var}")
plt.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

OOS benchmark comparison: does the baseline Phillips curve add forecasting value?¶

To assess forecasting power, we compare the baseline Phillips-curve OOS forecast to simple inflation-only benchmarks that use only lagged inflation information: a no-change forecast ($\hat{\pi}_t=\pi_{t-1}$), a trailing 4-quarter mean, and an AR(1) estimated on the pre-2020 training sample.

Result (baseline PC does not beat simple benchmarks).
Over the common evaluation window, the baseline Phillips curve performs substantially worse than persistence-based benchmarks. The AR(1) benchmark achieves the lowest RMSE (and the naïve no-change forecast is similarly strong), while the baseline Phillips curve has a much higher RMSE and a noticeably negative bias (systematic underprediction). The trailing 4-quarter mean performs worst.

What the plot shows.
The persistence benchmarks (AR(1) and no-change) track the 2021–2022 run-up and subsequent decline in inflation more closely than the baseline Phillips curve. In contrast, the Phillips-curve forecast is too “flat”: it fails to generate enough inflation during the surge and remains below actual inflation for much of 2022–2023.

Interpretation.
This benchmark test suggests that the baseline Phillips curve, as specified, has limited incremental forecasting content relative to very simple time-series rules. Therefore, a claim that “the Phillips curve failed to predict post-pandemic inflation” is not meaningful unless it is shown that a well-specified Phillips curve (e.g., with alternative expectations measures and/or cost-push controls) can outperform these standard benchmarks.

Test 2: Longer-window pseudo-OOS to show “COVID isn’t the only hard part” (or “COVID is uniquely hard”)¶

In [77]:
# --- Longer-window pseudo-OOS benchmark comparison ---

# -----------------------
# 1) Prepare time series with benchmark predictors
# -----------------------
ts = df_full[["period_q", "date", y_var]].sort_values("period_q").copy()

# Benchmarks based only on lagged inflation information
ts["pi_l1"]  = ts[y_var].shift(1)                    # naive no-change: pi_{t-1}
ts["pi_ma4"] = ts[y_var].shift(1).rolling(4).mean()  # trailing 4-quarter mean

def forecast_metrics(y_true, y_hat):
    err = y_hat - y_true
    return {
        "RMSE": float(np.sqrt(np.mean(err**2))),
        "MAE":  float(np.mean(np.abs(err))),
        "Bias": float(np.mean(err)),
        "N":    int(len(err))
    }

def eval_benchmarks_one_window(ts, y, train_start, train_end, test_start, test_end, label, hac_lags=4):
    """
    Evaluate three inflation-only benchmarks in a given train/test window:
      (i) naive no-change (pi_{t-1})
      (ii) trailing 4-quarter mean
      (iii) AR(1) estimated on the train window
    """
    # --- Train sample for AR(1)
    train = ts[(ts["period_q"] >= train_start) & (ts["period_q"] <= train_end)].copy()
    train = train.dropna(subset=[y, "pi_l1"])

    Xtr = sm.add_constant(train["pi_l1"])
    ytr = train[y]
    ar1 = sm.OLS(ytr, Xtr).fit(cov_type="HAC", cov_kwds={"maxlags": hac_lags})

    # --- Test/evaluation sample (needs lag-based forecasts available)
    test = ts[(ts["period_q"] >= test_start) & (ts["period_q"] <= test_end)].copy()
    test = test.dropna(subset=[y, "pi_l1", "pi_ma4"]).copy()

    # Forecasts
    test["f_naive"] = test["pi_l1"]
    test["f_ma4"]   = test["pi_ma4"]
    test["f_ar1"]   = ar1.predict(sm.add_constant(test["pi_l1"], has_constant="add"))

    # Metrics table
    rows = []
    for mname, col in [
        ("AR(1)", "f_ar1"),
        ("Naive no-change (pi_{t-1})", "f_naive"),
        ("Trailing mean (4q)", "f_ma4"),
    ]:
        met = forecast_metrics(test[y], test[col])
        rows.append({
            "window": label,
            "model": mname,
            **met
        })

    metrics_df = pd.DataFrame(rows).sort_values("RMSE")
    return metrics_df, test, ar1

# -----------------------
# 2) Define the three windows (Periods)
# -----------------------
W1 = dict(
    label="Train 1968–2007, Test 2008–2019",
    train_start=pd.Period("1968Q1", freq="Q"),
    train_end=pd.Period("2007Q4", freq="Q"),
    test_start=pd.Period("2008Q1", freq="Q"),
    test_end=pd.Period("2019Q4", freq="Q"),
)

W2 = dict(
    label="Train 1968–2019, Test 2020–2023Q3",
    train_start=pd.Period("1968Q1", freq="Q"),
    train_end=pd.Period("2019Q4", freq="Q"),
    test_start=pd.Period("2020Q1", freq="Q"),
    test_end=pd.Period("2023Q3", freq="Q"),
)

W3 = dict(
    label="Train 1968–2007, Test 2008–2023Q3",
    train_start=pd.Period("1968Q1", freq="Q"),
    train_end=pd.Period("2007Q4", freq="Q"),
    test_start=pd.Period("2008Q1", freq="Q"),
    test_end=pd.Period("2023Q3", freq="Q"),
)

windows = [W1, W2, W3]

# -----------------------
# 3) Run all windows and stack results
# -----------------------
all_metrics = []
tests = {}     # store the evaluation dataframes (for plots)
ar_models = {} # store AR(1) fitted models (optional)

for w in windows:
    met, test_df, ar1 = eval_benchmarks_one_window(
        ts, y_var, w["train_start"], w["train_end"], w["test_start"], w["test_end"], w["label"], hac_lags=4
    )
    all_metrics.append(met)
    tests[w["label"]] = test_df
    ar_models[w["label"]] = ar1

bench_3win = pd.concat(all_metrics, ignore_index=True)
bench_3win
Out[77]:
window model RMSE MAE Bias N
0 Train 1968–2007, Test 2008–2019 AR(1) 0.675686 0.422451 0.091374 48
1 Train 1968–2007, Test 2008–2019 Naive no-change (pi_{t-1}) 0.678798 0.419344 0.041369 48
2 Train 1968–2007, Test 2008–2019 Trailing mean (4q) 1.056227 0.739481 0.067181 48
3 Train 1968–2019, Test 2020–2023Q3 AR(1) 0.856432 0.700365 -0.153881 15
4 Train 1968–2019, Test 2020–2023Q3 Naive no-change (pi_{t-1}) 0.866855 0.717684 -0.133281 15
5 Train 1968–2019, Test 2020–2023Q3 Trailing mean (4q) 1.713730 1.402523 -0.449346 15
6 Train 1968–2007, Test 2008–2023Q3 AR(1) 0.722129 0.488061 0.038283 63
7 Train 1968–2007, Test 2008–2023Q3 Naive no-change (pi_{t-1}) 0.727993 0.490378 -0.000214 63
8 Train 1968–2007, Test 2008–2023Q3 Trailing mean (4q) 1.244687 0.897348 -0.055802 63
In [78]:
for label, d in tests.items():
    plt.figure(figsize=(10,4))
    plt.plot(d["date"], d[y_var], label="Actual", linewidth=2)
    plt.plot(d["date"], d["f_ar1"], label="AR(1)")
    plt.plot(d["date"], d["f_naive"], label="Naive (pi_{t-1})")
    plt.plot(d["date"], d["f_ma4"], label="Trailing mean (4q)")
    plt.axhline(0, linewidth=1)
    plt.title(f"Benchmark forecasts: {label}  |  target = {y_var}")
    plt.legend()
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

Forecasting-difficulty check: benchmark performance across multiple OOS windows¶

A common lesson from the inflation forecasting literature is that simple univariate benchmarks are hard to beat, and that conclusions from pseudo out-of-sample (OOS) comparisons can depend on the evaluation window. Atkeson and Ohanian popularized a simple “no-change” type benchmark for inflation forecasting, and subsequent work emphasizes that inflation can become harder (or easier) to forecast as the inflation process changes over time.
Accordingly, before diagnosing “Phillips curve failure,” we first establish how far simple benchmarks can go in predicting inflation in the post-pandemic episode. :contentReference[oaicite:1]{index=1}

We evaluate three inflation-only benchmark forecasts:

  • Naive no-change: $\hat{\pi}_t=\pi_{t-1}$
  • Trailing mean (4 quarters): $\hat{\pi}_t=\frac{1}{4}\sum_{j=1}^{4}\pi_{t-j}$
  • AR(1): $\pi_t=a+\rho\pi_{t-1}+u_t$ estimated on the training sample

We compute RMSE/MAE/bias in three windows:

  1. Train 1968–2007, test 2008–2019 (post-GFC era)
  2. Train 1968–2019, test 2020–2023Q3 (COVID inflation era)
  3. Train 1968–2007, test 2008–2023Q3 (long stress test)

This establishes a disciplined baseline: if simple persistence benchmarks perform very well (or if their performance varies sharply by window), then “failed to predict” should not be interpreted as uniquely about the Phillips curve without further evidence.

Forecasting-difficulty results: benchmark performance across three OOS windows (and limitations)¶

We evaluate three inflation-only benchmark forecasts—AR(1), naive no-change ($\hat{\pi}_t=\pi_{t-1}$), and a trailing 4-quarter mean—across three pseudo out-of-sample (OOS) windows. The goal is to establish how difficult inflation forecasting is even before asking whether the Phillips curve “failed.”

Key results.

  • Benchmarks are strong in the post-GFC era (2008–2019).
    Over 2008–2019 (N=48), AR(1) and the naive no-change forecast perform very similarly (lowest RMSEs), while the trailing mean performs substantially worse. This is consistent with inflation behaving like a low-persistence/low-volatility process where simple time-series rules are hard to beat.
  • The COVID inflation episode is much harder to forecast with simple rules (2020–2023Q3).
    In 2020–2023Q3 (N=15), AR(1) remains best among benchmarks, but overall errors are larger and the trailing mean performs very poorly. Importantly, the negative bias for AR(1) and the naive benchmark indicates that persistence-based rules tend to underpredict the inflation surge.
  • Long stress test (2008–2023Q3) confirms benchmark dominance but highlights regime dependence.
    Over 2008–2023Q3 (N=63), AR(1) and no-change again dominate the trailing mean, but performance is worse than in 2008–2019 alone, indicating that the inclusion of the post-pandemic regime materially changes forecast difficulty.

Interpretation for the project question.
These results establish a baseline: post-pandemic inflation is difficult to forecast even with simple time-series models, and benchmark performance depends on the evaluation window. Therefore, claims that “the Phillips curve failed to predict inflation” should be interpreted relative to (i) how hard the episode is to forecast in general, and (ii) whether Phillips-curve variants can add predictive value beyond these strong benchmarks.

Limitations and caveats (important for interpreting benchmark comparisons)¶

  1. Small post-pandemic evaluation sample (N=15).
    Forecast rankings in 2020–2023Q3 are based on a short window. Differences in RMSE can be noisy, and a few quarters can drive results. This is a key reason to also report longer-window evidence and to avoid over-interpreting small RMSE gaps.

  2. Pseudo-OOS and revised data.
    This is not a fully real-time forecasting exercise: we use revised inflation series and construct lagged predictors using the final dataset. Results are best interpreted as a diagnostic of predictability under a consistent data vintage rather than as a real-time forecasting competition.

  3. Horizon/target dependence.
    All results here are for the specific inflation target used (e.g., PCE y/y). Forecast difficulty and benchmark performance can change with alternative transformations (q/q annualized vs y/y) and alternative inflation measures (headline vs core). This motivates checking robustness across targets/horizons if space allows.

  4. Model simplicity and regime change.
    AR(1) and no-change implicitly assume a stable inflation process. The deterioration in performance when moving from 2008–2019 to 2020–2023 reflects regime change (pandemic shocks, reopening dynamics, energy, supply bottlenecks). Benchmark dominance does not imply “structure is irrelevant”; it shows that capturing these regime changes is the key forecasting challenge.

  5. Benchmark set is deliberately narrow.
    These are intentionally low-information models. The point is not that they are the best possible forecasts, but that they set a high bar: a Phillips curve specification should be judged by whether it improves meaningfully on these baselines.

Test C: Forecast horizon sensitivity (1-step vs 4-step / y/y vs q/q)¶

In [79]:
# -----------------------
# Test C: switch inflation target to QoQ annualized
# -----------------------
y_var_qoq = "pi_pce_qoq_ann"

# Prepare time series with benchmark predictors
ts_qoq = df_full[["period_q", "date", y_var_qoq]].sort_values("period_q").copy()

ts_qoq["pi_l1"]  = ts_qoq[y_var_qoq].shift(1)                    # naive: pi_{t-1}
ts_qoq["pi_ma4"] = ts_qoq[y_var_qoq].shift(1).rolling(4).mean()  # trailing 4q mean

def forecast_metrics(y_true, y_hat):
    err = y_hat - y_true
    return {
        "RMSE": float(np.sqrt(np.mean(err**2))),
        "MAE":  float(np.mean(np.abs(err))),
        "Bias": float(np.mean(err)),
        "N":    int(len(err))
    }

def eval_benchmarks_one_window(ts, y, train_start, train_end, test_start, test_end, label, hac_lags=4):
    # Train AR(1)
    train = ts[(ts["period_q"] >= train_start) & (ts["period_q"] <= train_end)].copy()
    train = train.dropna(subset=[y, "pi_l1"])

    Xtr = sm.add_constant(train["pi_l1"])
    ytr = train[y]
    ar1 = sm.OLS(ytr, Xtr).fit(cov_type="HAC", cov_kwds={"maxlags": hac_lags})

    # Test
    test = ts[(ts["period_q"] >= test_start) & (ts["period_q"] <= test_end)].copy()
    test = test.dropna(subset=[y, "pi_l1", "pi_ma4"]).copy()

    test["f_naive"] = test["pi_l1"]
    test["f_ma4"]   = test["pi_ma4"]
    test["f_ar1"]   = ar1.predict(sm.add_constant(test["pi_l1"], has_constant="add"))

    rows = []
    for mname, col in [
        ("AR(1)", "f_ar1"),
        ("Naive no-change (pi_{t-1})", "f_naive"),
        ("Trailing mean (4q)", "f_ma4"),
    ]:
        met = forecast_metrics(test[y], test[col])
        rows.append({"window": label, "model": mname, **met})

    metrics_df = pd.DataFrame(rows).sort_values("RMSE")
    return metrics_df, test, ar1

# Define the same three windows as before
W1 = dict(
    label="Train 1968–2007, Test 2008–2019",
    train_start=pd.Period("1968Q1", freq="Q"),
    train_end=pd.Period("2007Q4", freq="Q"),
    test_start=pd.Period("2008Q1", freq="Q"),
    test_end=pd.Period("2019Q4", freq="Q"),
)

W2 = dict(
    label="Train 1968–2019, Test 2020–2023Q3",
    train_start=pd.Period("1968Q1", freq="Q"),
    train_end=pd.Period("2019Q4", freq="Q"),
    test_start=pd.Period("2020Q1", freq="Q"),
    test_end=pd.Period("2023Q3", freq="Q"),
)

W3 = dict(
    label="Train 1968–2007, Test 2008–2023Q3",
    train_start=pd.Period("1968Q1", freq="Q"),
    train_end=pd.Period("2007Q4", freq="Q"),
    test_start=pd.Period("2008Q1", freq="Q"),
    test_end=pd.Period("2023Q3", freq="Q"),
)

windows = [W1, W2, W3]

# Run all windows and stack results
all_metrics_qoq = []
tests_qoq = {}

for w in windows:
    met, test_df, ar1 = eval_benchmarks_one_window(
        ts_qoq, y_var_qoq,
        w["train_start"], w["train_end"],
        w["test_start"], w["test_end"],
        w["label"],
        hac_lags=4
    )
    all_metrics_qoq.append(met)
    tests_qoq[w["label"]] = test_df

bench_3win_qoq = pd.concat(all_metrics_qoq, ignore_index=True)
bench_3win_qoq
Out[79]:
window model RMSE MAE Bias N
0 Train 1968–2007, Test 2008–2019 AR(1) 1.984947 1.210243 0.414309 48
1 Train 1968–2007, Test 2008–2019 Trailing mean (4q) 2.080019 1.298381 0.099168 48
2 Train 1968–2007, Test 2008–2019 Naive no-change (pi_{t-1}) 2.085460 1.289835 0.051758 48
3 Train 1968–2019, Test 2020–2023Q3 AR(1) 1.801179 1.366553 -0.178890 15
4 Train 1968–2019, Test 2020–2023Q3 Naive no-change (pi_{t-1}) 1.936437 1.429420 -0.080778 15
5 Train 1968–2019, Test 2020–2023Q3 Trailing mean (4q) 2.243043 1.975741 -0.273024 15
6 Train 1968–2007, Test 2008–2023Q3 AR(1) 1.945526 1.246122 0.299919 63
7 Train 1968–2007, Test 2008–2023Q3 Naive no-change (pi_{t-1}) 2.050961 1.323069 0.020202 63
8 Train 1968–2007, Test 2008–2023Q3 Trailing mean (4q) 2.119972 1.459657 0.010551 63
In [23]:
for label, d in tests_qoq.items():
    plt.figure(figsize=(10,4))
    plt.plot(d["date"], d[y_var_qoq], label="Actual", linewidth=2)
    plt.plot(d["date"], d["f_ar1"], label="AR(1)")
    plt.plot(d["date"], d["f_naive"], label="Naive (pi_{t-1})")
    plt.plot(d["date"], d["f_ma4"], label="Trailing mean (4q)")
    plt.axhline(0, linewidth=1)
    plt.title(f"Benchmark forecasts: {label}  |  target = {y_var_qoq}")
    plt.legend()
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

Test C: benchmark forecasting under an alternative inflation definition (q/q annualized)¶

Inflation forecasting results can depend on the choice of inflation definition. Year-on-year inflation is mechanically smoother (it averages over four quarters), while quarter-on-quarter annualized inflation places more weight on high-frequency movements and turning points.
To ensure our benchmark conclusions are not an artifact of using a smoothed inflation series, we repeat the three-window benchmark comparison using q/q annualized PCE inflation ($\pi_t^{q/q,ann}$). This helps clarify whether “forecasting difficulty” is primarily about high-frequency volatility versus medium-horizon inflation dynamics.

Test C conclusion: benchmark forecasting with q/q annualized inflation¶

Repeating the benchmark comparison using q/q annualized PCE inflation changes the level of forecast errors but reinforces the core message: inflation is structurally difficult to forecast in pseudo-OOS tests, especially around turning points and high-volatility episodes.

What changes relative to y/y inflation.
q/q annualized inflation is much noisier than y/y inflation, so RMSEs are mechanically larger in every window. This is expected because q/q annualized inflation places full weight on quarter-to-quarter fluctuations rather than smoothing over four quarters.

What does not change (main takeaway).

  • Across windows, simple persistence-based benchmarks remain highly competitive. The AR(1) benchmark remains best in all three windows (or very close to best), with the naive no-change benchmark typically second.
  • In the COVID window (2020–2023Q3), all benchmarks exhibit nontrivial bias and larger errors, consistent with the idea that the post-pandemic episode represents a regime change that is hard for low-information time-series rules to track.

Interpretation for the broader project.
This exercise strengthens the forecasting-difficulty argument: even before introducing Phillips-curve structure, the data indicate that benchmark performance depends on the evaluation window and the inflation definition. Therefore, a claim that “the Phillips curve failed to predict inflation after the pandemic” should be judged relative to a demanding baseline—simple persistence forecasts that already struggle when inflation dynamics change sharply. This motivates the next section: testing whether specific Phillips-curve ingredients (expectations measures, slack definitions, and cost/supply controls) add forecasting value beyond these strong benchmarks.

Summary: what OOS exercises imply about the difficulty of forecasting inflation¶

Before attributing post-pandemic forecast errors to a “breakdown” of the Phillips curve, we first assess how hard inflation is to forecast in pseudo out-of-sample (OOS) tests more generally.

Benchmark contest (COVID window).
In the 2020Q1–2023Q3 evaluation window, simple persistence-based time-series benchmarks—especially an AR(1) and a naive no-change forecast—perform very well and can outperform a baseline Phillips-curve forecast. This indicates that the post-pandemic episode is not only challenging for Phillips-curve specifications; it is challenging for low-information forecasting rules in general.

Window sensitivity (three OOS windows).
Benchmark performance is not stable across regimes. Forecast errors are materially smaller in the post-GFC low-inflation era (2008–2019) than in the COVID inflation era (2020–2023Q3), and a long stress-test window (2008–2023Q3) reflects the deterioration when the post-pandemic regime is included. This reinforces that pseudo-OOS conclusions are evaluation-window-dependent.

Inflation-definition sensitivity (y/y vs q/q annualized).
Repeating the benchmark exercises using q/q annualized inflation yields mechanically larger errors (as expected due to higher volatility) but preserves the main message: simple benchmarks remain highly competitive, and forecast difficulty increases sharply around the post-pandemic regime change.

Implication for the project question.
These results establish a demanding baseline for interpreting “Phillips curve failure”: post-pandemic inflation is difficult to forecast even with simple models, and OOS rankings depend on the window and inflation definition. Therefore, to make the claim that the Phillips curve “failed,” we need to show that well-motivated Phillips-curve variants (expectations measurement, slack definitions, and supply/cost controls) either (i) meaningfully improve OOS performance relative to these benchmarks or (ii) reveal instability specifically in the slack–inflation relationship rather than generic forecast difficulty.

In [80]:
# Recompute PC forecast for Panel A with Michigan mean and overwrite eval_df column
pc_A_mean = oos_eval(df_train, df_test_common, y_var,
                     ["exp_mich_1y_mean", "u_gap_nroust"], hac_lags=4)
pc_pred_A = pc_A_mean["dtest"][["date", "period_q", "yhat"]].rename(columns={"yhat": "pi_hat_pc_new"})

# Merge by period_q (or date); then replace pi_hat_pc
eval_df = eval_df.drop(columns=["pi_hat_pc"], errors="ignore").merge(
    pc_pred_A[["period_q", "pi_hat_pc_new"]],
    on="period_q",
    how="left"
).rename(columns={"pi_hat_pc_new": "pi_hat_pc"})

eval_df[["period_q", "pi_hat_pc"]].head()
Out[80]:
period_q pi_hat_pc
0 2020Q1 1.200827
1 2020Q2 1.528293
2 2020Q3 1.997811
3 2020Q4 1.656025
4 2021Q1 2.810217

Plot used in paper¶

In [ ]:
# --- prerequisites (already in the notebook)
# y_var, x_baseline, eval_df, df_full, oos_eval, tests

# -----------------------------
# Panel A: Train 1968–2019, Test 2020–2023Q3
# (You already built this as eval_df in the benchmark section)
# -----------------------------
assert "eval_df" in globals(), "eval_df not found. Run the benchmark comparison setup cell first."
panelA = eval_df.copy()

# -----------------------------
# Panel B: Train 1968–2007, Test 2008–2023Q3
# Benchmarks already stored in tests[...] from bench_3win.
# Add PC predictions and merge.
# -----------------------------
assert "tests" in globals(), "tests not found. Run the bench_3win cell first."

w3_label = "Train 1968–2007, Test 2008–2023Q3"
assert w3_label in tests, f"'{w3_label}' not found in tests. Check the exact window label."

panelB = tests[w3_label].copy()

# Build train/test dfs for the Phillips curve in this window
train_B = df_full[(df_full["period_q"] >= pd.Period("1968Q1", freq="Q")) &
                  (df_full["period_q"] <= pd.Period("2007Q4", freq="Q"))].copy()
test_B  = df_full[(df_full["period_q"] >= pd.Period("2008Q1", freq="Q")) &
                  (df_full["period_q"] <= pd.Period("2023Q3", freq="Q"))].copy()

pc_B = oos_eval(train_B, test_B, y_var, x_baseline, hac_lags=4)
pc_pred_B = pc_B["dtest"][["period_q", "date", "yhat"]].rename(columns={"yhat": "pi_hat_pc"})

# Merge PC into the benchmark dataframe (benchmarks are in panelB already)
panelB = panelB.merge(pc_pred_B, on=["period_q", "date"], how="left")

# Keep only rows where all plotted series exist (benchmarks need lags)
panelB = panelB.dropna(subset=[y_var, "pi_hat_pc", "f_naive", "f_ma4", "f_ar1"]).copy()

# -----------------------------
# Plot: two-panel tracking
# -----------------------------
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 4.5), sharey=True)

# Panel A
ax = axes[0]
ax.plot(panelA["date"], panelA[y_var], label="Actual", linewidth=2)
ax.plot(panelA["date"], panelA["pi_hat_pc"], label="Phillips curve (baseline)")
ax.plot(panelA["date"], panelA["pi_l1"], label="No-change (πₜ₋₁)")
ax.plot(panelA["date"], panelA["pi_ma4"], label="Trailing mean (4q)")
ax.plot(panelA["date"], panelA["pi_hat_ar1"], label="AR(1)")
ax.axhline(0, linewidth=1)
ax.set_title("Panel A: Train 1968–2019, Test 2020–2023Q3")
ax.legend(fontsize=8)

# Panel B
ax = axes[1]
ax.plot(panelB["date"], panelB[y_var], label="Actual", linewidth=2)
ax.plot(panelB["date"], panelB["pi_hat_pc"], label="Phillips curve (baseline)")
ax.plot(panelB["date"], panelB["f_naive"], label="No-change (πₜ₋₁)")
ax.plot(panelB["date"], panelB["f_ma4"], label="Trailing mean (4q)")
ax.plot(panelB["date"], panelB["f_ar1"], label="AR(1)")
ax.axhline(0, linewidth=1)
ax.set_title("Panel B: Train 1968–2007, Test 2008–2023Q3")
ax.legend(fontsize=8)

plt.suptitle("Figure 2: Baseline Phillips curve vs inflation-only benchmarks", y=1.02)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

print("Panel A N:", len(panelA), "| Panel B N:", len(panelB))

Panel A N: 15 | Panel B N: 63

Analysis Section 3: Considering different variants of the Phillips curve and existing literature¶

Minimal helper for different Phillips curve variants¶

In [26]:
def run_and_plot(name, y, x, df_train_use=df_train, df_test_use=df_test_common, hac_lags=4, show_error=True):
    out = oos_eval(df_train_use, df_test_use, y, x, hac_lags=hac_lags)
    m = out["metrics"]
    print(f"\n{name}")
    print("Spec:", f"{y} ~ " + " + ".join(x))
    print(f"Train N={m['train_N']} | Test N={m['test_N']} | RMSE={m['rmse']:.3f} | MAE={m['mae']:.3f} | Bias={m['bias']:.3f}")

    d = out["dtest"].copy()

    plt.figure(figsize=(10,4))
    plt.plot(d["date"], d[y], label="Actual", linewidth=2)
    plt.plot(d["date"], d["yhat"], label="Predicted (pre-2020 fit)")
    plt.axhline(0, linewidth=1)
    plt.title(name)
    plt.legend()
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

    if show_error:
        plt.figure(figsize=(10,3.2))
        plt.plot(d["date"], d["err"], label="Forecast error (yhat - y)")
        plt.axhline(0, linewidth=1)
        plt.title(f"{name}: forecast errors")
        plt.legend()
        plt.tight_layout()
        plt.show()

    return out

Helper for Section 3.
Section 3 compares multiple Phillips-curve variants using the same workflow: estimate on the pre-2020 sample, predict OOS over 2020Q1–2023Q3, report RMSE/MAE/bias, and plot actual vs predicted (and optionally forecast errors).
This helper keeps the notebook readable by avoiding repeated plotting/printing code.

Hazell-style supply control: add energy inflation¶

In [27]:
y = "pi_pce_yoy"
slack = "u_gap_nroust"
exp = "exp_mich_1y_mean" if "exp_mich_1y_mean" in df_full.columns else "exp_mich_1y_median"

pc_energy = run_and_plot(
    name="Variant 1 (Hazell-style): expectations + u-gap + energy inflation",
    y=y,
    x=[exp, slack, "pi_pce_energy_yoy"]
)

Variant 1 (Hazell-style): expectations + u-gap + energy inflation
Spec: pi_pce_yoy ~ exp_mich_1y_mean + u_gap_nroust + pi_pce_energy_yoy
Train N=204 | Test N=15 | RMSE=0.618 | MAE=0.509 | Bias=0.373

Phillips curve variant 1: Hazell-style energy control¶

Hazell’s comment argues that a key reason a simple pre-2020 Phillips curve appears to “fail” post-2020 is the presence of large cost-push/supply shocks, especially energy-price inflation—which are omitted in baseline specifications.
To test this, we augment the baseline expectations-augmented Phillips curve with PCE energy inflation: $$\pi_t = \alpha + \beta\,\pi^e_t + \kappa\,slack_t + \gamma\,\pi^{energy}_t + \varepsilon_t.$$

  • If adding energy inflation materially reduces the systematic underprediction during 2021–2022 (lower RMSE and a bias closer to zero), then part of the “Phillips curve forecasting failure” reflects omitted cost-push shocks rather than a breakdown in the slack–inflation mapping.
  • If the underprediction remains large even after controlling for energy inflation, then the explanation likely lies in expectations measurement, slack mismeasurement, nonlinearity, or parameter instability.

Variant 1 result (Hazell-style): adding energy inflation¶

Augmenting the expectations-augmented Phillips curve with PCE energy inflation substantially improves out-of-sample (2020Q1–2023Q3) performance relative to the baseline specification. Visually, the predicted series now closely tracks the 2021–2022 inflation surge and subsequent disinflation, whereas the baseline Phillips curve systematically underpredicted the surge.

The forecast-error plot confirms this: errors are much smaller in magnitude and exhibit far less persistent negative bias than in the baseline model. Quantitatively, RMSE/MAE fall materially, and the average bias moves closer to zero.

Interpretation.
This supports the view emphasized in Hazell’s comment: the apparent post-pandemic “failure” of a simple Phillips curve can reflect omitted cost-push shocks (here proxied by energy inflation), rather than a breakdown of the underlying slack–inflation relationship.

Bottlenecks/global supply proxy: adding import-price inflation¶

In [28]:
# -----------------------------
# PC Variant 2: Bottlenecks / global supply proxies
# -----------------------------
y = "pi_pce_yoy"
slack = "u_gap_nroust"

# Use your preferred expectations proxy (best from horse race)
exp = "exp_mich_1y_mean" if "exp_mich_1y_mean" in df_full.columns else "exp_mich_1y_median"

# Variant 2A: add import-price inflation
pc_import = run_and_plot(
    name="Variant 2A: expectations + u-gap + import deflator inflation",
    y=y,
    x=[exp, slack, "import_def_yoy"]
)

# Variant 2B (optional): add import prices + oil inflation
pc_import_oil = run_and_plot(
    name="Variant 2B: expectations + u-gap + import deflator + oil inflation",
    y=y,
    x=[exp, slack, "import_def_yoy", "oil_yoy"]
)

Variant 2A: expectations + u-gap + import deflator inflation
Spec: pi_pce_yoy ~ exp_mich_1y_mean + u_gap_nroust + import_def_yoy
Train N=204 | Test N=15 | RMSE=0.708 | MAE=0.591 | Bias=0.111

Variant 2B: expectations + u-gap + import deflator + oil inflation
Spec: pi_pce_yoy ~ exp_mich_1y_mean + u_gap_nroust + import_def_yoy + oil_yoy
Train N=180 | Test N=15 | RMSE=0.759 | MAE=0.620 | Bias=0.101

Variant 2: bottlenecks / global supply narrative (import prices and oil)¶

A prominent post-pandemic narrative attributes much of the inflation surge to global supply disruptions (“bottlenecks”) and input-cost shocks, rather than domestic slack alone. Papers in this vein emphasize disruptions in global production networks, shipping/logistics constraints, and sharp movements in traded-goods prices and commodities as key drivers of 2021–2022 inflation dynamics (e.g., Bai et al. 2024; di Giovanni et al., and related “bottlenecks” work). This channel is also conceptually aligned with Beaudry, Hou & Portier’s “broad-based supply shock” interpretation: many prices rose together because supply-side forces were widespread.

To operationalize this idea within a Phillips-curve forecasting framework (without attempting a full causal decomposition), we augment the expectations-augmented Phillips curve with import-price inflation as an observable proxy for global cost-push pressures, and optionally add oil inflation to capture commodity input costs: $$ \pi_t = \alpha + \beta\,\pi^e_t + \kappa\,slack_t + \gamma\,\pi_t^{import} \;(+\; \delta\,\pi_t^{oil}) + \varepsilon_t . $$

How to read the result.

  • If adding import-price inflation reduces the post-2021 underprediction (lower RMSE and bias closer to zero), this supports the view that omitted global supply/cost shocks contributed materially to the baseline OOS “failure.”
  • If the improvement is small relative to Variant 1 (energy), it suggests that the key omitted cost-push component captured in this dataset is primarily energy-related, with import prices adding limited incremental forecasting content.
  • If performance worsens or coefficients become unstable, that may reflect multicollinearity among supply proxies (energy/import/oil) or the difficulty of separating distinct supply channels in a short post-pandemic window.

Variant 2 results (bottlenecks proxy): import prices improve OOS fit¶

Adding import-price inflation (import deflator y/y) substantially improves post-pandemic out-of-sample performance relative to the baseline Phillips curve. In the 2020Q1–2023Q3 window, RMSE falls to 0.708 and the average forecast bias is small (+0.11pp), indicating that a global cost-push proxy captures an important component of the inflation surge that the baseline specification missed.

Including oil inflation in addition to import-price inflation does not improve performance (RMSE rises to 0.759) and reduces the usable training sample, suggesting limited incremental predictive content beyond import prices and/or multicollinearity among supply proxies in this short episode.

Interpretation.
Consistent with “bottlenecks / broad-based supply shock” narratives (e.g., Bai et al.; di Giovanni; and related work cited by Hazell), post-pandemic inflation dynamics appear to be strongly influenced by supply-side cost pressures that are not captured by slack and expectations alone. This supports the view that the baseline OOS “failure” reflects omitted supply shocks rather than a breakdown of the Phillips curve concept per se.

In [29]:
# -----------------------------
# PC Variant 3: Slack = tightness (log V/U)
# -----------------------------
y = "pi_pce_yoy"

# Preferred expectations proxy
exp = "exp_mich_1y_mean" if "exp_mich_1y_mean" in df_full.columns else "exp_mich_1y_median"

# Variant 3A: expectations + log(V/U)
pc_vu = run_and_plot(
    name="Variant 3A: expectations + log(V/U) tightness",
    y=y,
    x=[exp, "log_vu"]
)

# Variant 3B: tightness + Hazell-style energy (often a strong combo)
pc_vu_energy = run_and_plot(
    name="Variant 3B: expectations + log(V/U) + energy inflation",
    y=y,
    x=[exp, "log_vu", "pi_pce_energy_yoy"]
)

# Variant 3C: tightness + import prices (bottlenecks proxy)
pc_vu_import = run_and_plot(
    name="Variant 3C: expectations + log(V/U) + import deflator inflation",
    y=y,
    x=[exp, "log_vu", "import_def_yoy"]
)

Variant 3A: expectations + log(V/U) tightness
Spec: pi_pce_yoy ~ exp_mich_1y_mean + log_vu
Train N=204 | Test N=15 | RMSE=0.636 | MAE=0.566 | Bias=0.238

Variant 3B: expectations + log(V/U) + energy inflation
Spec: pi_pce_yoy ~ exp_mich_1y_mean + log_vu + pi_pce_energy_yoy
Train N=204 | Test N=15 | RMSE=0.563 | MAE=0.456 | Bias=0.305

Variant 3C: expectations + log(V/U) + import deflator inflation
Spec: pi_pce_yoy ~ exp_mich_1y_mean + log_vu + import_def_yoy
Train N=204 | Test N=15 | RMSE=0.625 | MAE=0.530 | Bias=-0.031

Variant 3: labor-market tightness as slack (V/U), with and without supply controls¶

Post-pandemic labor markets featured unusually high vacancies alongside relatively low unemployment, raising concerns that the unemployment gap may not measure effective slack well when matching frictions and shortages dominate. A related strand of post-pandemic Phillips curve work argues that inflation responds more to labor-market tightness (e.g., the vacancy–unemployment ratio) than to unemployment, and that the relationship can steepen when tightness is extreme (Ball et al.; Benigno & Eggertsson; and related work cited in Hazell’s comment).

To operationalize this idea, we replace the unemployment gap with $\log(V/U)$ as the slack proxy in the expectations-augmented Phillips curve: $$\pi_t = \alpha + \beta\,\pi^e_t + \kappa\,\log(V/U)_t + \varepsilon_t.$$ We then consider whether tightness adds predictive power once we also control for cost-push shocks (energy inflation or import-price inflation), since post-pandemic inflation plausibly reflects both labor tightness and supply-side pressures: $$\pi_t = \alpha + \beta\,\pi^e_t + \kappa\,\log(V/U)_t + \gamma Z_t + \varepsilon_t.$$

How to read the result.

  • If Variant 3A improves OOS fit relative to the unemployment-gap Phillips curve, it supports the view that unemployment was a mismeasured slack proxy post-COVID.
  • If Variant 3A alone does not help but Variant 3B/3C does, the interpretation is that tightness matters mainly after controlling for cost-push shocks (i.e., both channels are relevant).
  • If tightness performs no better than the unemployment-gap specifications even with supply controls, that weakens the tightness-based Phillips curve narrative in this reduced-form forecasting exercise.
In [82]:
# -----------------------------
# Nonlinearity test: threshold in tightness (log V/U)
# -----------------------------
y = "pi_pce_yoy"
exp = "exp_mich_1y_mean" if "exp_mich_1y_mean" in df_full.columns else "exp_mich_1y_median"

# Choose a tightness threshold beforehand
q = 0.75
vu_thresh = df_train["log_vu"].dropna().quantile(q)
print(f"Tightness threshold (train {int(q*100)}th pct): {vu_thresh:.3f}")

def add_tight_regime(df, vu_thresh):
    d = df.copy()
    d["tight_high"] = (d["log_vu"] >= vu_thresh).astype(float)
    d["log_vu_x_tight"] = d["log_vu"] * d["tight_high"]
    return d

df_train_nl = add_tight_regime(df_train, vu_thresh)
df_test_nl  = add_tight_regime(df_test_common, vu_thresh)

# NL-A: allow slope on log(V/U) to be steeper in high-tightness regime
# pi = a + b*exp + k1*log_vu + k2*(log_vu * 1{tight})
pc_nl_vu = run_and_plot(
    name="Variant 3D (Nonlinear slack): exp + log(V/U) + log(V/U)×1{tight}",
    y=y,
    x=[exp, "log_vu", "log_vu_x_tight"],
    df_train_use=df_train_nl,
    df_test_use=df_test_nl
)

# NL-B: same nonlinearity + energy (Hazell-style cost-push)
pc_nl_vu_energy = run_and_plot(
    name="Variant 3E (Nonlinear slack + energy): exp + log(V/U) + log(V/U)×1{tight} + energy",
    y=y,
    x=[exp, "log_vu", "log_vu_x_tight", "pi_pce_energy_yoy"],
    df_train_use=df_train_nl,
    df_test_use=df_test_nl
)

Tightness threshold (train 75th pct): -0.233

Variant 3D (Nonlinear slack): exp + log(V/U) + log(V/U)×1{tight}
Spec: pi_pce_yoy ~ exp_mich_1y_mean + log_vu + log_vu_x_tight
Train N=204 | Test N=15 | RMSE=1.004 | MAE=0.859 | Bias=0.827

Variant 3E (Nonlinear slack + energy): exp + log(V/U) + log(V/U)×1{tight} + energy
Spec: pi_pce_yoy ~ exp_mich_1y_mean + log_vu + log_vu_x_tight + pi_pce_energy_yoy
Train N=204 | Test N=15 | RMSE=1.088 | MAE=0.922 | Bias=0.901

Variants 3D–3E results: high-tightness threshold nonlinearity performs poorly¶

We test a “high-tightness nonlinearity” by allowing the slope on $\log(V/U)$ to differ when tightness exceeds a pre-2020 threshold (defined as the training-sample 75th percentile). The estimated threshold is $\log(V/U)=-0.233$ (i.e., $V/U\approx 0.79$), so the “high tightness” regime is not restricted to extreme labor-shortage conditions.

OOS performance deteriorates sharply.
Relative to the linear tightness Phillips curve (Variant 3A: RMSE = 0.636), the nonlinear threshold specification performs much worse:

  • Variant 3D (nonlinear tightness): RMSE = 1.004, Bias = +0.827
  • Variant 3E (nonlinear tightness + energy): RMSE = 1.088, Bias = +0.901

The large positive biases indicate systematic overprediction under this nonlinear specification, suggesting that the percentile-threshold interaction creates an unstable effective tightness slope in the post-pandemic window.

Interpretation.
In this dataset, the evidence favors a linear tightness-based slack measure (and standard cost-push controls) over a percentile-threshold nonlinearity. This does not rule out nonlinearity in principle, but it indicates that this particular empirical implementation does not improve forecasting performance in the post-pandemic period.

Next step (closer to Benigno–Eggertsson).
Because the 75th-percentile cutoff does not correspond to a clear labor-shortage regime, we next test a more economically interpretable threshold, $V/U\ge 1$ (equivalently, $\log(V/U)\ge 0$), which matches the “labor shortage” regime emphasized in Benigno & Eggertsson.

Minimal Benigno-Eggertsson-style nonlinear tightness Phillips curve¶

In [83]:
# -----------------------------
# Minimal Benigno–Eggertsson nonlinearity test:
# define "labor shortage" regime as V/U >= 1  <=>  log(V/U) >= 0
# -----------------------------
y = "pi_pce_yoy"
exp = "exp_mich_1y_mean" if "exp_mich_1y_mean" in df_full.columns else "exp_mich_1y_median"

vu_thresh_be = 0.0  # log(V/U) >= 0  <=>  V/U >= 1
print("Benigno–Eggertsson cutoff: log(V/U) >=", vu_thresh_be, " (i.e., V/U >= 1)")

def add_shortage_regime(df, vu_thresh):
    d = df.copy()
    d["shortage"] = (d["log_vu"] >= vu_thresh).astype(float)
    d["log_vu_x_shortage"] = d["log_vu"] * d["shortage"]
    return d

df_train_be = add_shortage_regime(df_train, vu_thresh_be)
df_test_be  = add_shortage_regime(df_test_common, vu_thresh_be)

# BE-A: nonlinear tightness using labor-shortage regime
pc_be_nl = run_and_plot(
    name="BE-min (Nonlinear tightness): exp + log(V/U) + log(V/U)×1{V/U≥1}",
    y=y,
    x=[exp, "log_vu", "log_vu_x_shortage"],
    df_train_use=df_train_be,
    df_test_use=df_test_be
)

# BE-B: same + energy control (since cost-push mattered)
pc_be_nl_energy = run_and_plot(
    name="BE-min (Nonlinear tightness + energy): exp + log(V/U) + log(V/U)×1{V/U≥1} + energy",
    y=y,
    x=[exp, "log_vu", "log_vu_x_shortage", "pi_pce_energy_yoy"],
    df_train_use=df_train_be,
    df_test_use=df_test_be
)

Benigno–Eggertsson cutoff: log(V/U) >= 0.0  (i.e., V/U >= 1)

BE-min (Nonlinear tightness): exp + log(V/U) + log(V/U)×1{V/U≥1}
Spec: pi_pce_yoy ~ exp_mich_1y_mean + log_vu + log_vu_x_shortage
Train N=204 | Test N=15 | RMSE=0.893 | MAE=0.743 | Bias=0.702

BE-min (Nonlinear tightness + energy): exp + log(V/U) + log(V/U)×1{V/U≥1} + energy
Spec: pi_pce_yoy ~ exp_mich_1y_mean + log_vu + log_vu_x_shortage + pi_pce_energy_yoy
Train N=204 | Test N=15 | RMSE=1.066 | MAE=0.898 | Bias=0.868

Minimal Benigno–Eggertsson test: labor-shortage regime defined by $V/U \ge 1$¶

Benigno & Eggertsson’s post-pandemic Phillips curve argument emphasizes a regime change when the labor market moves into “shortage” territory, where vacancies exceed unemployed workers. A natural empirical implication is that inflation may respond more strongly to tightness when $V/U$ is high, so the slope of a tightness-based Phillips curve may steepen once $V/U \ge 1$.

To align with this interpretation, we define a labor-shortage indicator using an economically interpretable cutoff: $$ I_t = 1\{V/U \ge 1\} \quad \Leftrightarrow \quad 1\{\log(V/U)\ge 0\}.$$ We then estimate: $$ \pi_t = \alpha + \beta\,\pi_t^e + \kappa_1\,\log(V/U)_t + \kappa_2\,(\log(V/U)_t\cdot I_t) + \varepsilon_t, $$ so that the effective tightness slope in shortage states is $\kappa_1+\kappa_2$. We also consider an augmented version including energy inflation as a cost-push proxy.

How to read the result.

  • If $\kappa_2>0$ and OOS RMSE falls relative to the linear tightness model, that supports a “steepening in shortage states” interpretation consistent with Benigno–Eggertsson.
  • If OOS performance does not improve (or worsens), then the main gains from tightness-based models in this dataset likely come from using $\log(V/U)$ linearly (and from cost-push controls), rather than from a sharp regime change at $V/U=1$.

Minimal Benigno–Eggertsson nonlinearity test: $V/U \ge 1$ regime does not improve OOS forecasts¶

To align with Benigno & Eggertsson’s “labor shortage regime” interpretation, we define a high-tightness indicator using the economically interpretable cutoff $V/U \ge 1$ (equivalently, $\log(V/U)\ge 0$) and allow the slope on tightness to change in this regime: $$ \pi_t = \alpha + \beta\,\pi_t^e + \kappa_1\,\log(V/U)_t + \kappa_2\,(\log(V/U)_t\cdot 1\{V/U\ge 1\}) + \varepsilon_t. $$

Result.
This regime-switching specification performs substantially worse than the linear tightness Phillips curve in the post-pandemic OOS window:

  • Nonlinear tightness (no energy): RMSE = 0.893, Bias = +0.702
  • Nonlinear tightness + energy: RMSE = 1.066, Bias = +0.868

The large positive biases indicate systematic overprediction, and RMSE increases sharply relative to the linear tightness specifications. Thus, within this reduced-form forecasting exercise, the data do not support the view that introducing a sharp kink at $V/U=1$ improves predictive performance. The gains from tightness-based Phillips curves in this dataset appear to come from using $\log(V/U)$ linearly (and from including cost-push controls), rather than from a shortage-regime nonlinearity.

Slope stability test (post-2020 interaction): does the slack–inflation relationship change?¶

In [ ]:
# Use full sample up to 2023Q3 (consistent with your test window choice)
df_all = df_full[df_full["period_q"] <= pd.Period("2023Q3", freq="Q")].copy()

def slope_stability_test(df, y, exp_var, slack_var, controls, post_var="post_covid", hac_lags=4, name=""):
    """
    Estimate y ~ exp + slack + controls + slack*post
    Report implied pre and post slack slopes.
    """
    d = df.copy()
    d["slack_post"] = d[slack_var] * d[post_var].astype(float)

    xvars = [exp_var, slack_var, "slack_post"] + list(controls)
    use = d[[y, "period_q"] + xvars].dropna().copy()

    X = sm.add_constant(use[xvars])
    Y = use[y]
    res = sm.OLS(Y, X).fit(cov_type="HAC", cov_kwds={"maxlags": hac_lags})

    kappa_pre = res.params[slack_var]
    delta = res.params["slack_post"]
    kappa_post = kappa_pre + delta

    # SE for post slope = Var(kappa_pre + delta)
    cov = res.cov_params()
    var_post = cov.loc[slack_var, slack_var] + cov.loc["slack_post", "slack_post"] + 2*cov.loc[slack_var, "slack_post"]
    se_post = float(np.sqrt(var_post))

    out = pd.DataFrame({
        "model": [name],
        "N": [len(use)],
        "kappa_pre": [kappa_pre],
        "se_pre": [res.bse[slack_var]],
        "delta_post": [delta],
        "se_delta": [res.bse["slack_post"]],
        "kappa_post": [kappa_post],
        "se_post": [se_post],
        "p_delta": [res.pvalues["slack_post"]],
    })
    return res, out

y = "pi_pce_yoy"
exp = "exp_mich_1y_mean" if "exp_mich_1y_mean" in df_all.columns else "exp_mich_1y_median"

# Spec A: Hazell-style u-gap + energy
res_u_energy, tab_u_energy = slope_stability_test(
    df=df_all,
    y=y,
    exp_var=exp,
    slack_var="u_gap_nroust",
    controls=["pi_pce_energy_yoy"],
    name="Stability: exp + u-gap + energy (+ u-gap×post)"
)

# Spec B: tightness + energy  (best-performing linear tightness spec)
res_vu_energy, tab_vu_energy = slope_stability_test(
    df=df_all,
    y=y,
    exp_var=exp,
    slack_var="log_vu",
    controls=["pi_pce_energy_yoy"],
    name="Stability: exp + log(V/U) + energy (+ log(V/U)×post)"
)

stability_table = pd.concat([tab_u_energy, tab_vu_energy], ignore_index=True)
stability_table
Out[ ]:
model N kappa_pre se_pre delta_post se_delta kappa_post se_post p_delta
0 Stability: exp + u-gap + energy (+ u-gap×post) 219 0.037146 0.110562 -0.110566 0.112239 -0.073420 0.036922 0.324577
1 Stability: exp + log(V/U) + energy (+ log(V/U)... 219 -0.131704 0.302387 0.138723 0.439434 0.007019 0.309087 0.752241

print(res_vu_energy.summary())

Capstone test: did the Phillips curve slope change after 2020?¶

Improved post-pandemic fit can come from adding cost-push proxies (energy/import prices) or using alternative slack measures (tightness), but this does not automatically imply that the slack–inflation relationship itself remained stable. To directly test whether the Phillips curve mechanism changed after COVID, we allow the slope on slack to differ post-2020 by interacting slack with a post-pandemic indicator:

$$ \pi_t = \alpha + \beta\,\pi_t^e + \kappa\,slack_t + \delta\,(slack_t \times \mathbf{1}\{t\ge 2020\text{Q1}\}) + \gamma Z_t + \varepsilon_t. $$

Here, $\kappa$ is the pre-2020 slope and $\kappa+\delta$ is the post-2020 slope. We implement this test for two leading specifications motivated by the literature and our forecasting results: (i) unemployment-gap slack with an energy control (Hazell-style), and (ii) tightness slack $\log(V/U)$ with an energy control.

  • If $\delta$ is small (and statistically insignificant), this supports the interpretation that the Phillips curve did not structurally break; baseline forecast errors primarily reflected omitted shocks/mismeasured slack rather than a changed slope.
  • If $\delta$ is large and significant, that indicates a post-2020 slope shift even after controlling for energy inflation—consistent with a genuine change in the slack–inflation relationship.

Capstone result: little evidence of a post-2020 slope break once cost-push is controlled for¶

To test whether the Phillips curve “broke” in the sense of a changed slack–inflation slope, we augment the preferred specifications with a post-2020 interaction: $$ \pi_t=\alpha+\beta\pi_t^e+\kappa\,slack_t+\delta\,(slack_t\times \mathbf{1}\{t\ge 2020\text{Q1}\})+\gamma\,\pi_t^{energy}+\varepsilon_t, $$ so that $\kappa$ is the pre-2020 slope and $\kappa+\delta$ is the post-2020 slope.

Result. In both the unemployment-gap and tightness ($\log(V/U)$) specifications, the post-2020 interaction term is not statistically significant (p-values ≈ 0.33 and 0.75 respectively). Thus, conditional on an energy cost-push proxy and survey expectations, we do not find strong evidence that the Phillips curve slope changed after 2020.

Interpretation. Taken together with the large OOS improvements from adding energy/import price controls and using tightness as slack, this supports the view that the apparent post-pandemic “failure to predict” largely reflects omitted cost-push shocks and slack measurement issues rather than a clear structural break in the underlying Phillips-curve mechanism (noting that slope estimates are imprecise and the post-2020 window is short).

Robustness checks for Section 3¶

In [85]:
# -----------------------------
# Robustness R1: Cross-era OOS validation
# -----------------------------
# Two evaluation windows:
#   Window A: Train 1968–2007, Test 2008–2019
#   Window B: Train 1968–2019, Test 2020–2023Q3
#
# Uses your existing oos_eval(df_train, df_test, y, x, hac_lags=4)

def make_window(df, start_q, end_q):
    return df[(df["period_q"] >= pd.Period(start_q, freq="Q")) &
              (df["period_q"] <= pd.Period(end_q, freq="Q"))].copy()

# Define windows (consistent with your earlier Section 2 window logic)
train_A = make_window(df_full, "1968Q1", "2007Q4")
test_A  = make_window(df_full, "2008Q1", "2019Q4")

train_B = make_window(df_full, "1968Q1", "2019Q4")
test_B  = make_window(df_full, "2020Q1", "2023Q3")

# Preferred expectations proxy
exp = "exp_mich_1y_mean" if "exp_mich_1y_mean" in df_full.columns else "exp_mich_1y_median"

# Define the three Section 3 specs you want to validate
specs = [
    ("PC u-gap + energy (Hazell)", [exp, "u_gap_nroust", "pi_pce_energy_yoy"]),
    ("PC u-gap + import (bottlenecks)", [exp, "u_gap_nroust", "import_def_yoy"]),
    ("PC log(V/U) + energy (tightness)", [exp, "log_vu", "pi_pce_energy_yoy"]),
]

def eval_specs_in_window(y, train_df, test_df, window_label, specs, hac_lags=4):
    rows = []
    for name, x in specs:
        out = oos_eval(train_df, test_df, y, x, hac_lags=hac_lags)
        m = out["metrics"]
        rows.append({
            "window": window_label,
            "y": y,
            "spec": name,
            "train_N": m["train_N"],
            "test_N": m["test_N"],
            "RMSE": m["rmse"],
            "MAE": m["mae"],
            "Bias": m["bias"],
        })
    return pd.DataFrame(rows)

y_target = "pi_pce_yoy"

r1_A = eval_specs_in_window(y_target, train_A, test_A, "Train 1968–2007, Test 2008–2019", specs)
r1_B = eval_specs_in_window(y_target, train_B, test_B, "Train 1968–2019, Test 2020–2023Q3", specs)

r1_table = pd.concat([r1_A, r1_B], ignore_index=True)
r1_table.sort_values(["window", "RMSE"])
Out[85]:
window y spec train_N test_N RMSE MAE Bias
2 Train 1968–2007, Test 2008–2019 pi_pce_yoy PC log(V/U) + energy (tightness) 156 48 1.574350 1.270801 1.222823
0 Train 1968–2007, Test 2008–2019 pi_pce_yoy PC u-gap + energy (Hazell) 156 48 1.621781 1.357406 1.356819
1 Train 1968–2007, Test 2008–2019 pi_pce_yoy PC u-gap + import (bottlenecks) 156 48 1.789675 1.488528 1.487857
5 Train 1968–2019, Test 2020–2023Q3 pi_pce_yoy PC log(V/U) + energy (tightness) 204 15 0.562787 0.455831 0.305249
3 Train 1968–2019, Test 2020–2023Q3 pi_pce_yoy PC u-gap + energy (Hazell) 204 15 0.617802 0.509203 0.373498
4 Train 1968–2019, Test 2020–2023Q3 pi_pce_yoy PC u-gap + import (bottlenecks) 204 15 0.708073 0.591115 0.111105

Robustness R1: cross-era validation (motivated by window sensitivity in Section 2)¶

Section 2 showed that inflation forecasting performance is strongly evaluation-window dependent: the post-pandemic period is unusually difficult to forecast even for simple benchmarks. To ensure that the Section 3 “rescued Phillips curve” specifications are not merely COVID-specific patches, we re-evaluate our three leading Phillips-curve variants in an earlier out-of-sample window.

We compare OOS performance in two windows:

  1. Train 1968–2007, test 2008–2019 (post-GFC low-inflation era)
  2. Train 1968–2019, test 2020–2023Q3 (post-pandemic inflation era)

Each specification uses the same expectations proxy and differs only in the slack proxy and/or the cost-push control (energy vs import prices). If the augmented specifications improve fit in both windows (or at least do not deteriorate in the earlier window), that supports the interpretation that they reflect a more robust NKPC-style specification rather than an episode-specific adjustment.

Robustness R1 result: cross-era validation highlights strong regime dependence¶

Re-estimating and evaluating the leading Section 3 Phillips-curve variants across two OOS windows reveals pronounced regime dependence.

In the post-pandemic window (2020Q1–2023Q3), the augmented specifications perform well (e.g., tightness+energy RMSE ≈ 0.56; u-gap+energy RMSE ≈ 0.62; u-gap+import RMSE ≈ 0.71), consistent with the view that incorporating cost-push proxies and/or tighter slack measures can explain much of the baseline “failure to predict.”

However, when evaluated in the post-GFC low-inflation era (2008–2019), the same specifications perform poorly and exhibit large positive biases (≈ +1.2 to +1.5pp), implying systematic overprediction of inflation. This suggests that these Phillips-curve variants capture the post-pandemic inflation regime but do not generalize to the earlier anchored-inflation regime without further modeling of trend inflation/expectations anchoring or additional mechanisms.

Interpretation.
Combined with Section 2’s window-sensitivity evidence, this cross-era exercise implies that pseudo-OOS “success” in 2020–2023 should not be interpreted as identifying a universally stable Phillips curve. Instead, the evidence points to instability across monetary/inflation regimes: post-pandemic inflation dynamics are better matched by specifications with cost-push proxies and tightness measures, whereas the post-GFC era remains difficult to reconcile in the same reduced-form framework.

In [86]:
# -----------------------------
# R1b: Cross-era validation using SPF 1-year expectations instead of Michigan
# -----------------------------
import pandas as pd

def make_window(df, start_q, end_q):
    return df[(df["period_q"] >= pd.Period(start_q, freq="Q")) &
              (df["period_q"] <= pd.Period(end_q, freq="Q"))].copy()

train_A = make_window(df_full, "1968Q1", "2007Q4")
test_A  = make_window(df_full, "2008Q1", "2019Q4")

train_B = make_window(df_full, "1968Q1", "2019Q4")
test_B  = make_window(df_full, "2020Q1", "2023Q3")

# Use SPF 1-year expectations (must exist)
exp_spf = "exp_spf_1y"
assert exp_spf in df_full.columns, "exp_spf_1y not found. Check transforms/column names."

specs_spf = [
    ("PC u-gap + energy (Hazell) [SPF1y]", [exp_spf, "u_gap_nroust", "pi_pce_energy_yoy"]),
    ("PC u-gap + import (bottlenecks) [SPF1y]", [exp_spf, "u_gap_nroust", "import_def_yoy"]),
    ("PC log(V/U) + energy (tightness) [SPF1y]", [exp_spf, "log_vu", "pi_pce_energy_yoy"]),
]

def eval_specs_in_window(y, train_df, test_df, window_label, specs, hac_lags=4):
    rows = []
    for name, x in specs:
        out = oos_eval(train_df, test_df, y, x, hac_lags=hac_lags)
        m = out["metrics"]
        rows.append({
            "window": window_label,
            "y": y,
            "spec": name,
            "train_N": m["train_N"],
            "test_N": m["test_N"],
            "RMSE": m["rmse"],
            "MAE": m["mae"],
            "Bias": m["bias"],
        })
    return pd.DataFrame(rows)

y_target = "pi_pce_yoy"

r1b_A = eval_specs_in_window(y_target, train_A, test_A, "Train 1968–2007, Test 2008–2019", specs_spf)
r1b_B = eval_specs_in_window(y_target, train_B, test_B, "Train 1968–2019, Test 2020–2023Q3", specs_spf)

r1b_table_spf = pd.concat([r1b_A, r1b_B], ignore_index=True)

# Display sorted within window
r1b_table_spf.sort_values(["window", "RMSE"])
Out[86]:
window y spec train_N test_N RMSE MAE Bias
0 Train 1968–2007, Test 2008–2019 pi_pce_yoy PC u-gap + energy (Hazell) [SPF1y] 106 48 0.304998 0.234575 -0.007524
2 Train 1968–2007, Test 2008–2019 pi_pce_yoy PC log(V/U) + energy (tightness) [SPF1y] 106 48 0.356723 0.287739 -0.072168
1 Train 1968–2007, Test 2008–2019 pi_pce_yoy PC u-gap + import (bottlenecks) [SPF1y] 106 48 0.372729 0.281537 0.158088
3 Train 1968–2019, Test 2020–2023Q3 pi_pce_yoy PC u-gap + energy (Hazell) [SPF1y] 154 15 1.746672 1.441684 -1.420438
5 Train 1968–2019, Test 2020–2023Q3 pi_pce_yoy PC log(V/U) + energy (tightness) [SPF1y] 154 15 1.904800 1.619557 -1.619557
4 Train 1968–2019, Test 2020–2023Q3 pi_pce_yoy PC u-gap + import (bottlenecks) [SPF1y] 154 15 2.071303 1.731859 -1.577464

Robustness (expectations proxy): SPF expectations flip the cross-era results¶

Repeating the cross-era OOS validation using SPF 1-year expectations (instead of Michigan) yields a striking reversal.

In the post-GFC low-inflation era (2008–2019), Phillips-curve variants with SPF expectations perform extremely well (RMSE ≈ 0.30–0.37) and exhibit little bias. This suggests that professional expectations were well aligned with realized inflation in the anchored regime, and that earlier overprediction using Michigan expectations reflected a mismatch between household expectations and realized inflation in this period.

However, in the post-pandemic window (2020–2023Q3), the SPF-based specifications perform very poorly (RMSE ≈ 1.75–2.07) with large negative biases (≈ −1.4 to −1.6pp), implying systematic underprediction of the inflation surge. In contrast, the Michigan-based specifications fit the post-pandemic window far better.

Interpretation.
These results imply that conclusions about “Phillips curve failure” are highly sensitive to the measurement of expected inflation. Professional expectations appear better suited to the low-inflation/anchored regime, while household expectations better capture the re-anchoring/unanchoring dynamics of the post-pandemic episode. This motivates treating expectations measurement as a central identification issue rather than a minor robustness choice.

In [87]:
# -----------------------------
# Robustness R2: swap supply proxy (energy vs oil vs gasoline)
# -----------------------------
y = "pi_pce_yoy"
exp = "exp_mich_1y_mean" if "exp_mich_1y_mean" in df_full.columns else "exp_mich_1y_median"
slack = "log_vu"

# OOS window: train pre-2020, test 2020–2023Q3 (your main COVID window)
df_train_R2 = df_train
df_test_R2  = df_test_common

supply_proxies = [
    ("PCE energy inflation", "pi_pce_energy_yoy"),
    ("Brent oil inflation", "oil_yoy"),
    ("Gas price inflation", "gas_price_yoy"),
]

rows = []
outs = {}

for label, z in supply_proxies:
    name = f"R2: exp + {slack} + {label}"
    out = oos_eval(df_train_R2, df_test_R2, y, [exp, slack, z], hac_lags=4)
    m = out["metrics"]
    rows.append({
        "supply_proxy": label,
        "z_var": z,
        "train_N": m["train_N"],
        "test_N": m["test_N"],
        "RMSE": m["rmse"],
        "MAE": m["mae"],
        "Bias": m["bias"],
    })
    outs[label] = out

r2_table = pd.DataFrame(rows).sort_values("RMSE")
r2_table
Out[87]:
supply_proxy z_var train_N test_N RMSE MAE Bias
0 PCE energy inflation pi_pce_energy_yoy 204 15 0.562787 0.455831 0.305249
1 Brent oil inflation oil_yoy 180 15 0.631994 0.543500 0.061613
2 Gas price inflation gas_price_yoy 108 15 1.341489 1.026187 -0.970238
In [37]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

for label, out in outs.items():
    d = out["dtest"].copy()
    plt.figure(figsize=(10,4))
    plt.plot(d["date"], d[y], label="Actual", linewidth=2)
    plt.plot(d["date"], d["yhat"], label="Predicted (pre-2020 fit)")
    plt.axhline(0, linewidth=1)
    plt.title(f"R2 supply-proxy swap: {label}")
    plt.legend()
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

Robustness R2: does the “supply control rescues fit” result depend on the specific proxy?¶

To check that our Section 3 conclusions are not driven by a single energy measure, we re-estimate the same Phillips-curve specification while swapping alternative supply/cost proxies available in the dataset:

  • PCE energy inflation (direct energy component of the price index),
  • Brent oil inflation (global crude price proxy),
  • gasoline price inflation (consumer-facing energy price proxy).

We hold fixed the expectations proxy and slack measure, and evaluate each variant out-of-sample over 2020Q1–2023Q3. If results are robust across these proxies, it strengthens the interpretation that the post-pandemic forecast errors reflect an omitted cost-push/supply-shock component rather than an idiosyncratic measurement choice.

Robustness R2: does the “supply control rescues fit” result depend on the specific proxy?¶

To check that our Section 3 conclusions are not driven by a single energy measure, we re-estimate the same Phillips-curve specification while swapping alternative supply/cost proxies available in the dataset:

  • PCE energy inflation (direct energy component of the price index),
  • Brent oil inflation (global crude price proxy),
  • gasoline price inflation (consumer-facing energy price proxy).

We hold fixed the expectations proxy and slack measure, and evaluate each variant out-of-sample over 2020Q1–2023Q3. If results are robust across these proxies, it strengthens the interpretation that the post-pandemic forecast errors reflect an omitted cost-push/supply-shock component rather than an idiosyncratic measurement choice.

In [88]:
# -----------------------------
# Robustness R3: use core inflation target (core PCE)
# -----------------------------
y_core = "pi_corepce_yoy"
exp = "exp_mich_1y_mean" if "exp_mich_1y_mean" in df_full.columns else "exp_mich_1y_median"
slack = "log_vu"

# Ensure core target exists
assert y_core in df_full.columns, "pi_corepce_yoy not found. Check transforms / column names."

# Compare a small ladder of specs on the core target
specs_core = [
    ("Core PC: exp + log(V/U)", [exp, slack]),
    ("Core PC: exp + log(V/U) + energy", [exp, slack, "pi_pce_energy_yoy"]),
    ("Core PC: exp + log(V/U) + import", [exp, slack, "import_def_yoy"]),
    ("Core PC: exp + log(V/U) + oil", [exp, slack, "oil_yoy"]),
]

rows = []
outs_core = {}

for name, x in specs_core:
    out = oos_eval(df_train, df_test_common, y_core, x, hac_lags=4)
    m = out["metrics"]
    rows.append({
        "model": name,
        "train_N": m["train_N"],
        "test_N": m["test_N"],
        "RMSE": m["rmse"],
        "MAE": m["mae"],
        "Bias": m["bias"],
    })
    outs_core[name] = out

r3_core_table = pd.DataFrame(rows).sort_values("RMSE")
r3_core_table
Out[88]:
model train_N test_N RMSE MAE Bias
2 Core PC: exp + log(V/U) + import 204 15 0.592885 0.485386 0.192858
0 Core PC: exp + log(V/U) 204 15 0.596508 0.495105 0.160161
1 Core PC: exp + log(V/U) + energy 204 15 0.651971 0.504852 0.131050
3 Core PC: exp + log(V/U) + oil 180 15 0.670309 0.560059 0.035703
In [39]:
# pick the best 2 rows by RMSE
best_models = r3_core_table["model"].head(2).tolist()

for mname in best_models:
    d = outs_core[mname]["dtest"].copy()
    plt.figure(figsize=(10,4))
    plt.plot(d["date"], d[y_core], label="Actual core PCE (y/y)", linewidth=2)
    plt.plot(d["date"], d["yhat"], label="Predicted (pre-2020 fit)")
    plt.axhline(0, linewidth=1)
    plt.title(f"R3 core target: {mname}")
    plt.legend()
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

Robustness R3 result: core inflation behaves differently (import matters more than energy)¶

Repeating the key tightness-based Phillips-curve specifications using core PCE inflation yields a clear contrast with the headline results. The linear tightness Phillips curve performs well for core inflation (RMSE ≈ 0.60), and adding import-price inflation slightly improves fit further (RMSE ≈ 0.59). In contrast, adding energy inflation does not improve performance for core inflation (RMSE rises to ≈ 0.65), consistent with the fact that core PCE excludes direct energy components.

Interpretation.
This strengthens the Section 3 conclusion by showing that improvements from supply proxies are not purely mechanical: energy controls primarily help explain headline inflation dynamics, whereas import-price inflation provides the more relevant supply-side proxy for core inflation (consistent with broader bottleneck/traded-goods cost channels).

For core PCE, adding energy inflation does not improve OOS performance, while adding import-price inflation yields at most a modest improvement. The main predictive content for core inflation comes from expectations and the tightness measure itself, consistent with the idea that energy controls primarily matter for headline inflation.

Section 3 conclusion: Phillips curve variants, nonlinearity, stability, and robustness¶

This section evaluated whether the apparent post-pandemic “Phillips curve failure” reflects (i) misspecification/omitted shocks, (ii) slack mismeasurement, or (iii) a structural change in the slack–inflation relationship. We estimate each specification on the pre-2020 sample and evaluate out-of-sample (OOS) forecasts over 2020Q1–2023Q3, motivated by post-pandemic Phillips curve debates.

1) Cost-push/supply controls substantially reduce the baseline OOS miss (headline inflation).
Adding a Hazell-style energy inflation control or a bottlenecks-style import-price proxy materially improves OOS fit for headline PCE inflation relative to the baseline expectations+slack specification. Both results support the interpretation that a large part of the post-pandemic forecast error reflects omitted cost-push/supply shocks shifting the Phillips curve, rather than a breakdown of the concept itself.

2) Slack measurement matters: tightness (log V/U) outperforms unemployment slack in the pandemic window.
Replacing the unemployment gap with labor-market tightness $\log(V/U)$ improves OOS performance, consistent with the view that unemployment is an incomplete measure of effective slack when vacancies are unusually elevated. Combining tightness with a supply proxy (especially energy inflation) yields the strongest headline forecasting performance in the pandemic window.

3) Nonlinearity (kinked/slanted-L) specifications do not improve forecasting performance here.
Motivated by “steepening in very tight labor markets” arguments, we tested threshold nonlinearity using (i) a training-sample high-tightness cutoff and (ii) an economically interpretable labor-shortage regime cutoff $V/U\ge 1$ (Benigno–Eggertsson style). In both cases, the nonlinear interaction specifications worsen OOS performance and introduce large positive biases. In this quarterly dataset and forecasting design, the evidence favors linear tightness specifications over kinked regime-switching forms.

4) Capstone stability check: no clear evidence of a post-2020 slope break once cost-push is controlled for.
Allowing the slack coefficient to differ post-2020 via a $slack \times post$ interaction yields no statistically significant slope shift in either the unemployment-gap or tightness-based specification (conditional on energy inflation and survey expectations). This supports the view that the post-pandemic “failure” is primarily about omitted shocks and slack/expectations measurement rather than a clear structural collapse of the slack–inflation slope (noting that estimates are imprecise and the post-2020 window is short).

5) Robustness: regime dependence and expectations measurement are central.
A cross-era OOS validation shows that the post-pandemic “best” specifications do not automatically generalize to 2008–2019 when household expectations are used (large overprediction), while using SPF 1-year expectations produces excellent performance in 2008–2019 but large underprediction in 2020–2023Q3. This highlights that conclusions about Phillips curve “failure” are highly sensitive to how expected inflation is measured and that expectation formation appears regime-dependent.

6) Robustness: alternative supply proxies and core inflation.
Swapping supply proxies shows that headline results are robust to using Brent oil inflation as a cost-push proxy (with somewhat weaker accuracy but much smaller bias), while gasoline inflation performs poorly largely due to limited/noisy sample coverage. Using core PCE inflation as the target, energy inflation no longer improves fit (consistent with “not just energy explains energy”), while import-price inflation provides at most a modest improvement; for core inflation, most predictive content comes from expectations and tightness rather than energy.

Overall takeaway (Section 3).
The post-pandemic Phillips curve did not “fail” in an absolute sense: once we use a tightness-based slack proxy and include plausible cost-push controls, pre-2020 estimates can track post-2020 inflation much more closely. The evidence points to a specification/measurement problem—especially the role of supply shocks and expectations proxies—rather than strong evidence of a kinked Phillips curve or a statistically clear post-2020 slope break.

In [ ]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# -----------------------------
# Exhibit 3: Table of Phillips curve variants (OOS 2020Q1–2023Q3)
# Metrics: RMSE / MAE / Bias
# Plus slack slope: kappa (HAC s.e.) and standardized kappa * sd(slack)
# Note: df_full, df_train, df_test_common, and oos_eval should be defined in earlier cells
# -----------------------------

y = "pi_pce_yoy"
exp = "exp_mich_1y_mean" if "exp_mich_1y_mean" in df_full.columns else "exp_mich_1y_median"

# Ensure we have a baseline *consistent with Section 3* (Michigan mean if available)    
pc_base_mean = oos_eval(df_train, df_test_common, y, [exp, "u_gap_nroust"], hac_lags=4)

def extract_row(name, out, slack_var):
    m = out["metrics"]
    res = out["res"]
    dtr = out["dtrain"]
    
    kappa = float(res.params.get(slack_var, np.nan))
    se_kappa = float(res.bse.get(slack_var, np.nan))
    sd_slack = float(dtr[slack_var].std(ddof=1)) if slack_var in dtr.columns else np.nan
    kappa_std = kappa * sd_slack if np.isfinite(kappa) and np.isfinite(sd_slack) else np.nan
    
    return {
        "Model": name,
        "Slack": slack_var,
        "Train N": m["train_N"],
        "Test N": m["test_N"],
        "RMSE": m["rmse"],
        "MAE": m["mae"],
        "Bias": m["bias"],
        "kappa": kappa,
        "SE(kappa)": se_kappa,
        "kappa × sd(slack)": kappa_std,
    }

# Build the model list from objects you already created in Section 3
models = [
    ("Baseline: exp + u-gap", pc_base_mean, "u_gap_nroust"),
    ("V1 Hazell: + energy", pc_energy, "u_gap_nroust"),
    ("V2A Bottlenecks: + import", pc_import, "u_gap_nroust"),
    # include if you want; otherwise comment out
    ("V2B: + import + oil", pc_import_oil, "u_gap_nroust"),
    ("V3A Tightness: log(V/U)", pc_vu, "log_vu"),
    ("V3B Tightness + energy", pc_vu_energy, "log_vu"),
    ("V3C Tightness + import", pc_vu_import, "log_vu"),
]

rows = [extract_row(n, out, slack) for n, out, slack in models]
tab3 = pd.DataFrame(rows)

# Format for display
tab3_disp = tab3.copy()
for c in ["RMSE","MAE","Bias","kappa","SE(kappa)","kappa × sd(slack)"]:
    tab3_disp[c] = tab3_disp[c].map(lambda v: np.nan if pd.isna(v) else float(v))
tab3_disp = tab3_disp.sort_values("RMSE").reset_index(drop=True)

# Round
tab3_disp_rounded = tab3_disp.copy()
tab3_disp_rounded[["RMSE","MAE","Bias","kappa","SE(kappa)","kappa × sd(slack)"]] = \
    tab3_disp_rounded[["RMSE","MAE","Bias","kappa","SE(kappa)","kappa × sd(slack)"]].round(3)

tab3_disp_rounded

Model Slack Train N Test N RMSE MAE Bias kappa SE(kappa) kappa × sd(slack)
0 V3B Tightness + energy log_vu 204 15 0.563 0.456 0.305 -0.082 0.316 -0.038
1 V1 Hazell: + energy u_gap_nroust 204 15 0.618 0.509 0.373 0.035 0.111 0.050
2 V3C Tightness + import log_vu 204 15 0.625 0.530 -0.031 -0.183 0.343 -0.084

In [ ]:
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

# ---- choose your baseline expectations proxy
y = "pi_pce_yoy"
exp = "exp_mich_1y_mean" if "exp_mich_1y_mean" in df_full.columns else "exp_mich_1y_median"

# ensure a consistent baseline object (recompute once)
pc_base = oos_eval(df_train, df_test_common, y, [exp, "u_gap_nroust"], hac_lags=4)

# ---- helper formatters
def fmt(x, nd=3):
    return "" if pd.isna(x) else f"{float(x):.{nd}f}"

def coef_se(res, var, nd=3):
    if var not in res.params:
        return "", ""
    return fmt(res.params[var], nd), f"({fmt(res.bse[var], nd)})"

def build_col(out, slack_var):
    m = out["metrics"]
    res = out["res"]
    b, se = coef_se(res, slack_var, 3)
    return {
        "coef": b,
        "se": se,
        "RMSE": fmt(m["rmse"], 3),
        "Bias": fmt(m["bias"], 3),
        "Train N": str(int(m["train_N"])),
        "Test N": str(int(m["test_N"])),
    }

models = [
    # Panel A: unemployment gap specs
    ("Baseline\n(exp + u-gap)",      pc_base,        "u_gap_nroust", "A"),
    ("+ Energy\n(Hazell)",           pc_energy,      "u_gap_nroust", "A"),
    ("+ Import\n(bottlenecks)",      pc_import,      "u_gap_nroust", "A"),
    # ("+ Import + Oil",             pc_import_oil,  "u_gap_nroust", "A"),

    # Panel B: tightness specs
    ("Tightness\n(exp + log V/U)",   pc_vu,          "log_vu",       "B"),
    ("Tightness\n+ Energy",          pc_vu_energy,   "log_vu",       "B"),
    ("Tightness\n+ Import",          pc_vu_import,   "log_vu",       "B"),
]

# ---- build column blocks
cols_A = [(name, out, slack) for name, out, slack, panel in models if panel == "A"]
cols_B = [(name, out, slack) for name, out, slack, panel in models if panel == "B"]

def make_block(cols, block_title):
    """
    Returns a DataFrame formatted as:
      row1: Slack slope
      row2: (SE)
      then RMSE, Bias, Train N, Test N
    """
    data = {}
    for name, out, slack_var in cols:
        data[name] = build_col(out, slack_var)

    # row order
    rows = [
        ("Slack slope", "coef"),
        ("", "se"),
        ("RMSE", "RMSE"),
        ("Bias", "Bias"),
        ("Train N", "Train N"),
        ("Test N", "Test N"),
    ]

    block = pd.DataFrame(
        {col: [data[col][key] for _, key in rows] for col in data.keys()},
        index=[r for r, _ in rows]
    )

    # Add a panel header row (will be inserted later in LaTeX)
    return block, block_title

blockA, titleA = make_block(cols_A, "Panel A: Unemployment-gap Phillips curves")
blockB, titleB = make_block(cols_B, "Panel B: Tightness Phillips curves (log V/U)")

# ---- combine blocks with blank line between (for readability in notebook)
combined = pd.concat([blockA, pd.DataFrame([[""]*blockA.shape[1]], columns=blockA.columns, index=[""]), blockB])
combined
Out[ ]:

(0.105) (0.111) (0.126) NaN NaN NaN
RMSE 0.633 0.618 0.708 NaN NaN NaN
Bias 0.267 0.373 0.111 NaN NaN NaN

Test N 15 15 15 NaN NaN NaN
NaN NaN NaN

NaN NaN NaN (0.318) (0.316) (0.343)
